name: Better Half Coffee & Cocktails
open_for: Breakfast, lunch, brunch, dinner; Breakfast, lunch, dinner
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: A few blocks west of Downtown, Better Half keeps things cool with an all-day menu and a solid cheeseburger for $12; during happy hour from 3 p.m. to 6 p.m. Tuesday through Friday, the burger’s price comes down to a very affordable $7. It’s the kind of cheeseburger that doesn’t need fuss: juicy patty, crisp vegetables, Dijonnaise, and a toasty bun, best paired with a side of cauliflower tots or french fries. | The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This all-day cafe was named Eater Austin’s restaurant of 2018, and still, the team, which includes co-owners Matt and Grady Wright and Matthew Bolick, continues to deliver fantastic service and convivial vibes. The West Fifth space feels appropriately Austin with its large patio shaded by an old live oak tree, great coffee, and a first-rate lineup of beer, wine, and cocktails. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The Clarksville-adjacent all-day restaurant has a patio and menu that are highly adaptable for any occasion: first date, networking meeting, or post-bike brunch. Try one of the best breakfast sandwiches in town with an iced coffee under the shade of a centuries-old live oak tree, or come late and meet new people over cocktails and burgers and hearty salads on the communal benches, painted a friendly shade of pink. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Better Half blurs the line between coffee shop, diner, and all-day hangout in the West Fifth corridor. Weekday lunch, served from 8 a.m. to 3 p.m. in a counter-service set-up, brings heavy hitters like a double cheeseburger, cauliflower tots, and lighter bites, including salads.
tags: burgers; eater-38; essential; lunch; outdoor-dining; patio
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Dai Due
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Over in Cherrywood, Dai Due takes the Texas burger to another level, and it sometimes comes with slight variations. The huge $26 house burger blends dry-aged beef, then tops it with cheese, fancy mayo of some kind, and crispy pickles, onions, or jalapeños. It’s rich, earthy, and worth the relatively higher price point, especially with a glass of natural wine on the shaded patio. | The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This Cherrywood Texan restaurant is so regionally minded that even beer and wine are exclusively from the state. Equally satisfying for a lingering brunch or blowout feast at dinner, the Texas-forever menu under chef Jesse Griffith has heavier dishes like dry-aged wagyu rib-eyes, quails stuffed with boudin noir, grilled chicken hearts, and coffee-cured antelope leg filets. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: What’s a butcher shop without offering steak on its Texas-forever menu? This Cherrywood restaurant, which recently received a Michelin Bib Gourmand, prides itself on sourcing its meats locally and seasonally. The dining room and patio may be slightly barebones, but the bar seating overlooks the open kitchen, with plenty of action to observe. Sip a Texas-based wine to pair with your expertly cooked steak. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Seasonal ingredients sourced across Texas define Dai Due. Game meats, pork chops, and charcuterie boards — which can feature anything from antelope salami to Wagyu beef bologna to chicken liver mousse — show chef Jesse Griffiths’s commitment to local products. A rustic butcher shop setting paired with attentive service and Texas wines makes it a memorable dining experience.
tags: burgers; eater-38; essential; neighborhood-guide; steak; steakhouse; ut-campus
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Steakhouses in Austin; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Bartlett’s
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Forget fighting Downtown parking or waiting for reservations. The steaks at this underrated North Shoal Creek restaurant include filet mignon and a rib-eye marinated in a pineapple-sesame-ginger sauce. Those in search of the more elusive prime rib will be excited to see it on the lunch and dinner menu. Slide into a dimly lit booth or saddle up to the traditional wooden bar for classic martinis to accompany your beef. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Lunch is served daily from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at this North Shoal Creek steakhouse, with a menu that includes prime rib sandwiches, sashimi tuna salad, and steak frites. Inside, it has dark wood, leather booths, and quiet sophistication — an ideal backdrop for business lunches, long conversations, or simply hiding out for an hour. The martinis don’t hurt, either. | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: This longtime American restaurant in North Shoal Creek gives off classic vibes with big cozy booths, a small outdoor area with a fireplace, and warm hospitality. It’s an excellent place for a burger or steak and a martini, and there’s a strong selection of gluten-free options.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Habanero Cafe
address: 501 W Oltorf St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: In a modest house on Oltorf near South First, Habanero Café delivers classic Tex-Mex with no frills and full flavor. From 11 a.m. to 3 p.m., find daily lunch specials like enchiladas, crispy taco plates, and chicken mole done right. The dining room is small but bustling — expect a short wait and plenty of regulars. | The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: This little daytime Dawson Tex-Mex restaurant has all the staples (fajitas, quesadillas, enchiladas), but the specialties are truly boss. The El Patron huevos rancheros come with two fried eggs smothered in ranchero sauce, all next to a pile of fajitas and steaming refried beans. | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: The little family-owned Mexican restaurant in Dawson is a beloved dining choice by those in the know. For almost 30 years, the chori-migas plate and menudo have been go-tos for anyone needing to recover from a night out. The small, homey space also offers terrific enchiladas, fajitas, quesadillas, and more. Beware of the fiery habanero salsa — it is no joke.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Hopfields
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: On Guadalupe in West Campus, Hopfields is a French-ish gastropub with a surprisingly luxe burger. The Pascal features a juicy patty, Camembert cheese, and whole-grain mustard on a brioche bun, with a side of pommes frites for $23 during lunch, brunch, or dinner. Make it fancy with a glass or bottle of wine, or more casual with a pick from the extensive list of more than 30 draft beers. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: While the menu at this French Heritage neighborhood gastropub doesn’t offer multiple beef options, the steak frites are a standout. It offers a choice of filet mignon drenched in butter and Dijon or a hanger steak au poivre, both served with a heap of the famous Hopfields’ fries. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: French-inspired fare and an impressive drink list draw crowds to Hopfields. The front bar feels relaxed and communal, while the dining room offers a more sophisticated spot to enjoy refined European plates. The signature burger and the mussels and frites are favorites, but the escargot dumplings are a must-order. Ditto the fries.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The Best Steakhouses in Austin; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Jacoby's Restaurant & Mercantile
address: 3235 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: This rustic-chic Govalle restaurant has one of the most relaxing patios in town. Enjoy views of a quiet and pretty section of the Colorado River with refreshing cocktails and burgers that feature beef from the owner’s ranch. There’s an indoor dining room as well. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: This Govalle Southern restaurant raises its own brand of beef on the Jacoby Ranch, which is used for steaks that are later dry-aged from 21 to 28 days. The chicken-fried steak and burger are staples, but the daily butcher’s cut, paired with mashed potatoes, broccolini, and black garlic fried onions, should not be missed. Make brunch or dinner a breezy occasion by dining on the spacious, rustic patio. | Austin Restaurants With Great Views: The grassy outdoor area of the Texan restaurant in Govalle boasts a peaceful view of the Colorado River through a canopy of trees. Jacoby’s uses beef from the family’s ranch, so the steaks and burgers are popular. It also makes for an aesthetically pleasing brunch, with mimosas served in fancy carafes and French Toast made from blueberry bread.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Steakhouses in Austin; Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Jeffrey’s
address: 1204 W Lynn St, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Jeffrey’s is a landmark Austin restaurant for a few reasons. It opened in 1975 as one of the first fine-dining restaurants in the city. In 2013 it got a facelift as an early project of Larry McGuire and Tom Moorman, now MML Hospitality, and cemented the group’s status as the adjudicator of cool. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: This Clarksville institution is ideal for a romantic dinner date or special occasion, so plan to make reservations. Start with a classic martini before mulling over the slew of prime beef steak options of angus or dry-aged wagyu. Snag an intimate booth and save room (and extra time) for a decadent souffle. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: This Clarksville fine-dining institution was reimagined by the entrepreneurs at McGuire Moorman Hospitality in 2013 (now McGuire Moorman Lambert), transforming it into an ideal venue for special occasions, with wine dinners hosted throughout the year and the option for private dining in the Napoleon Room and The Apartment.
tags: eater-38; essential; fine-dining; special-occasion; steak; steakhouse
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Steakhouses in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Justine’s
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This East Austin French restaurant enshrines indulgence with rich dishes, late hours, and a festive atmosphere. Open until midnight on weekends, Justine’s serves French food like steak frites, escargot, duck confit, and ratatouille, along with a hefty burger topped with Gruyere cheese. There’s a blood-red dining room, but most seating is on the patio, which is draped in fabric and lit by chandeliers in the winter. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Follow in the steps of every celebrity that comes through town and head to this French restaurant known for its late hours, hedonistic pleasures, and vivacious spirit. In the winter, the patio is covered with a tent and maximally decorated to become its own luxe destination. The menu offers dishes like escargot, duck confit, and a rich burger. A word of warning: Make a reservation or be prepared to wait. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Known for its festive outdoor winter tent and romantic vibes, Justine’s also serves a classic French steak frites dish. A 12-ounce grass-fed, wood-grilled rib-eye is plated with crispy fries, and a choice of sauce completes the fun atmosphere at this Govalle restaurant. Start with the escargots, and choose a funky French bottle of red to round out the meal.
tags: eater-38; essential; outdoor-dining; patio; steak; steakhouse
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Kiin Di
address: 2414 S Lamar Blvd Suite A, Austin, TX 78704
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Popular Thai food truck Kiin Di outgrew its trailer at Corner Bar and opened a full restaurant just down the street on South Lamar in January. Joining favorites like creamy curry crab and flaky roti are new dishes like pork ribs kati cooked in coconut milk along with cocktails. | The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Once a food truck at Corner Bar, beloved Thai restaurant Kiin Di has opened in its own space with a larger menu. Find fare like roti with a curried dipping sauce, gai tod (traditional Thai fried chicken served on its own or with ginger rice and pickled cucumbers), and stir-fried noodles. | 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: In Zilker, Kiin Di operates out of a food truck parked at Corner Bar, serving creamy soft-shell crab curry, Thai-style fried chicken, and pad Thai. Co-owners and chefs Panyada Chaikantha, Bee TP, and Chris Matthana focus on balanced flavors that shine in every dish. The casual bar patio complements the food with a lively, street-food energy.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin; The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin; 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar
address: 6405 Brodie Lane, Sunset Valley, Texas 78745, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar is a new five-acre beauty in Sunset Valley from the teams behind the Thai food truck Dee Dee and Mexican restaurant Veracruz All Natural. Leona marks Dee Dee’s first-ever brick-and-mortar location, serving their beloved pad kapow alongside coffee from the Australian-based Proud Mary, with drinks like a banana milk hojicha and Thai pumpkin latte. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: South Austin’s latest patio destination has everything: an inventive cocktail list, excellent food options from Dee Dee Thai and Veracruz Tacos, a play area for kids, and a beautiful, sprawling outdoor area with fire pits and a stream running through the space. There are over 80 species of plants in the garden, which means the flowers and foliage will change with the seasons. | The Best Coffee in Austin: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar is a new five-acre beauty in Sunset Valley from the teams behind the Thai food truck Dee Dee and Mexican restaurant Veracruz All Natural. Leona marks Dee Dee’s first-ever brick-and-mortar location, serving their beloved pad kapow alongside coffee from the Australian-based Proud Mary with drinks like a banana milk hojicha and Thai pumpkin latte.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Sour Duck Market
address: 1814 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Of the Bryce Gilmore restaurants, this casual Blackland cafe, which is the center of the restaurant group’s baking operations, is the one with the best patio situation. The large outdoor space has misters, fans, shaded areas, sunny areas, and a chalkboard trailer for kids to play in. Try the wagyu smash burger, which is a steal during happy hour, along with excellent pastries, coffee, and cocktails. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Located between Cherrywood and East Austin proper, Sour Duck Market is an easygoing spot where the line moves quickly, and the lunch hits the mark. Think breakfast sandwiches, double cheeseburgers, fish ‘n’ chips, and seasonal salads, including the recent version with field greens, maple mustard vinaigrette, shaved radish, strawberries, fennel, mole granola, and queso fresco. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Part cafe, part beer garden, Sour Duck Market draws regulars with burgers, pastries, and brunch favorites. The patio and communal seating encourage long visits, whether for weekend gatherings or weekday lunches. Seasonal touches keep the menu fresh and engaging. Don’t skip the smoked chicken, and always take home a baked good, even if you’re stuffed.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Asahi Imports
address: 3005 S Lamar Blvd Suite B-105B, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: The South Austin location of the low-key Japanese market is the place to go for tasty onigiri, bento boxes, and soft serve. The Japanese konbini-style aisles make for a fun browsing and to-go experience (this is the place to grab lunch for today and tomorrow). Speed up your visit by ordering onigiri or bento boxes for takeout. | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: With a location in Brentwood and another in South Lamar, this Japanese market secretly serves amazing Japanese food to go. Aside from flavorful onigiri, Asahi dishes out filling bento boxes with mains like chicken karaage, fried shrimp, and miso salmon, plus an array of incredible imported snacks to peruse like Pocky, chips, and candy.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Austin Rotisserie & Sandwicherie
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: In Downtown’s bustling Fareground food hall, Austin Rotisserie stands out with its focused French menu and unfussy execution. Rotisserie chicken comes plated with golden potatoes or layered into baguette sandwiches alongside herbed mayo and greens. Indoor and shaded outdoor seating make this spot ideal for everything from lunch-hour meetings to post-trail bites. Grab a café au lait macaron for later. | Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: This Downtown food stall, located at the Fareground food hall, turns out some of the best baguettes in town — crisp on the outside and tender inside. Several sandwiches feature its signature roasted chicken, topped with house-made roti sauce, while others include ingredients such as ham and salted butter, duck with pâté, or tuna with olives and egg. The menu leans French but stays casual and approachable.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Birdie’s
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This Chestnut neighborhood wine bar offers a friendly, casual dining experience with James Beard Award-recognized food and beverages. Every month, co-owner and chef Tracy Malechek-Ezekiel creates a new seven-course prix fixe menu of simple dishes done well. Expect handmade pasta, minute steak with a rotating vegetable like smoked cabbage, and wine-friendly seasonal appetizers like fried green tomatoes. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Need a patio for a nice date night out? The no-reservations counter-service wine restaurant in Chestnut has received nods from Michelin, the James Beard Awards (for excellent food and beverage service), and Eater Austin for its excellent pastas, salads, and bistro fare that prioritizes locally sourced ingredients. The patio out back has seating options for groups, intimate dates, or solo evenings out.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Bouldin Creek Café
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Beloved by residents of 78704 and beyond, this vegetarian cafe in Bouldin Creek from owner Leslie Martin offers a varied menu of diner fare paired with locally roasted coffee. The cafe provides a taste of old-school Austin in its design (a quirky Slackers vibe inside and out) and food, which features loaded vegetable taco plates with sides of crispy plantain chips. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Bouldin Creek Café has long defined South First with its South Austin quirk and sprawling, meat-free menu. Standouts include the tofu BLT and rotating veggie stir-fries, served in a mural-covered space that stays busy. It’s open from 8 a.m. until 10 p.m. (11 p.m. on weekends), and it works at nearly any hour. The coffee runs strong enough to power through the afternoon slump.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Bufalina Due
open_for: Dinner; Dinner daily, lunch on weekends
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Owner Steven Dilley and his pizzeria teams continue to impress diners at this Brentwood restaurant, which has an East Austin location with even more pasta options. The Neapolitan pies are simple but skillfully executed. Diners can’t go wrong with the exemplary margherita and the various special pies, which have included zucchini or potato pizzas, or appetizers like the Caesar salad or burrata with juicy peaches. | The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Sister to Bufalina, this East Austin restaurant slings expertly executed Neapolitan pies that have earned raves from critics and diners alike. Try different combinations, like the margherita; the fresca, a refreshing pie topped with mozzarella, prosciutto, arugula, lemon, and Parmesan; or the potato pie, which features crème fraiche, lots of garlic, and a cured egg yolk.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Canje
must_try: The boldly spicy jerk chicken. It’s unforgettable.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The East Austin Caribbean restaurant from the talented Emmer & Rye restaurant group was named Eater Austin’s best new restaurant in 2022, and it continues to stack accolades, including a James Beard Award recognition. Canje pays tribute to executive chef and pastry expert Tavel Bristol-Joseph’s Guyanese roots with buttery roti, wild boar pepper pot, and jerk chicken. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: A hit from the Emmer & Rye team, this vibrant East Austin location celebrates Caribbean flavors with standout dishes like wild boar pepperpot and Wagyu beef suya. The cocktail menu is just as transportive, featuring drinks like the Frozen Guava Colada and Mango Palmer. While there’s no formal tasting menu, the rotating specials and “suppa” mains make each visit feel curated.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Casino El Camino
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: A Downtown dive through and through, Casino El Camino cranks out monster burgers — three-quarters of a pound of charbroiled Angus beef, to be exact. Priced between $15 and $18, these burgers are messy, meaty, and unapologetically over-the-top. | The Best Dive Bars in Austin: With burgers, eardrum-shattering metal from the jukebox, and affordable drinks served across a sticky bar, this is pretty much a textbook Downtown dive. Located on Sixth Street, it offers a dimly lit, no-nonsense escape from the usual Downtown chaos, complete with cult-favorite bloody marys. Outdoors, a small patio is inexplicably decorated like a Mayan temple—perfect for enjoying that gigantic burger.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Choo Sando
address: 5222 Burnet Rd Ste 535, Austin, TX, 78756, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: While most people know this counter-service Rosedale restaurant for its great Japanese sandwiches, its sushi box selection shouldn’t be overlooked. The array often features exquisite pieces of fish such as bluefin tuna, salmon, and scallops. There are a variety of lunch sushi boxes available as grab-and-go to eat in the small, cheery restaurant or its few outdoor tables (or order online). | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: This unassuming Japanese cafe in Rosedale focuses on two items done beautifully well: sushi boxes and adorable Japanese sandwiches on fluffy milk bread offered in savory and sweet varieties. Try the tamago, a combination of egg salad and soft-boiled eggs; the katsu sandwich stuffed with panko-covered pork chop and slaw; or the classic strawberry with whipped cream, which makes for a light dessert.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Craft Omakase
address: 4400 North Lamar Boulevard, Suite 102, Austin, Texas 78756
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: This Rosedale restaurant is Austin’s first sushi restaurant to earn a Michelin star. Run by Uchiko alums, the Japanese restaurant serves omakase only and focuses on letting ingredients shine through its nigiri, small dishes, and drinks. Think octopus with a touch of citrus or bluefin tuna topped with a dollop of caviar. Book reservations online for the uber-sleek indoor omakase counter. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Within a year of opening, the Rosedale omakase restaurant — launched by Uchiko alum Charlie Wang and Nguyen Nguyen — earned a Michelin star, quickly becoming one of the city’s finest Japanese tasting menu destinations. The $175 22-course meal centers on minimalistic presentation, pristine nigiri, small plates, and excellent hospitality. Book reservations online for indoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Crown & Anchor Pub
address: 2911 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: A North Campus favorite since 1987, this beloved neighborhood pub nails the dive-bar burger formula. No bells and whistles — just a grilled patty made up of fresh, never frozen double-grinded beef and melted Swiss, American, or cheddar cheeses, plus add-ons like jalapeños, bacon, and mushrooms. Pair with fries, with the optional addition of melted cheese and ranch dressing, and a cold pint on the side. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Since opening in 1987, Crown & Anchor Pub has earned a loyal following of students, neighbors, and longtime regulars. Burgers and chili dogs anchor the menu, best paired with a rotating lineup of local and craft beers. There are also darts and pool tables to keep the night going.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Dirty Martin’s Place
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Dirty Martin’s A West Campus institution since 1926, Dirty Martin’s is all grease, grit, and nostalgia, in the best way possible. Priced between $7 and $11, the burgers come smashed and sizzling off the flat-top, wrapped in wax paper with crispy edges and a soft, squishy bun. There’s no pretense here — just classic diner fare and a patio full of regulars, students, and the occasional hungover professor. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Generations of students have gathered at Dirty Martin’s Place, which has served the Drag since 1926 and still boasts a no-fills, retro interior that preserves the joint’s history. The menu features classic burgers and hearty sandwiches complemented by hand-spun milkshakes.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: El Naranjo
address: 2717 S Lamar Blvd STE 1085, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: The chef and co-owner of the South Lamar Oaxacan restaurant, Iliana de la Vega, won a James Beard Award in 2022 for good reason: Her food is outstanding, from the perfect mole negro de Oaxaca with duck breast to the weekly ceviche. Find it all in a bright, modern dining room with sunflower-yellow walls. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Helmed by James Beard Award-winning chef Iliana de la Vega, this South Lamar Mexican restaurant serves up refined Oaxacan cuisine that’s soulful and sophisticated. Duck mole negro and tlayuda oaxaqueña are signature dishes; most of the menu is gluten-free. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Este
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This Cherrywood restaurant from the team behind Suerte is the ultimate destination for quality coastal Mexican food in Austin — that’s precisely why the team, led by Fermín Núñez, won an Eater Award in 2023. Every dish guarantees big flavor: There’s messy camarones “el ricas” (buttery spicy grilled shelled shrimp), creamy salsa-drenched pan-seared halibut, and fresh raw bar offerings. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Seafood lovers can rejoice in the bold and playful menu at the Blackland Mexican restaurant. Chef Fermín Núñez crafts bold, fantastic, flavorful, and fresh dishes, such as the camarones “el ricas,” which are messy fun in the best way, and a decadent seafood tower featuring clams preparados and morita-drawn butter lobsters. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Fabrik
address: 1701 East Martin Luther King Jr Boulevard, Unit 102, Austin, TX 78702
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: At this fine-dining tasting menu restaurant in the Blackland neighborhood, co-owner and chef Je Wallerstein shows the delicate power of high-end vegan cuisine. The seven-course tasting menu is full of creative plates that pull from her Japanese, Italian, and Nordic training. This can mean seasonal dishes like springy focaccia with vegan butter and bean dip, and potatoes paired with smoked ricotta and mushrooms. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Cuisine inspired by the seasons is the calling card for this vegan fine dining spot situated in the Blackland neighborhood. Through seven courses, chef and owner Je Wallerstein executes incredibly thoughtful dishes in an effort to “showcase the diversity of plant-based cuisine.” The house-made garlic “butter” is considered a standout.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Feral Pizza
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ You have to plan a little to get a pie from Feral Pizza, a trailer tucked away in Hyde Park that’s only open from 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. Thursday through Sunday, but it’s worth it. The sourdough crust on these NY-style pizzas is sharply tangy, and crispy on the bottom. | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: Ordering online from this gas station-adjacent pizza truck in North Loop is essential, as the garlic-laden sourdough pizzas often sell out. But, the advance planning is worth it, as the husband-and-wife team running the trailer put extra love into finishing each crispy pizza with ingredients like creamy ricotta, scratch-made sausage, or homemade ranch.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Fish Shop
address: 1401 East 6th Street, #201, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: This East Sixth seafood joint brings a laid-back, California coastal sensibility to the workday lunch crowd, open weekdays starting at 11:30 a.m. The menu is comforting and indulgent, with the “secret” Filet O’ Fish sandwich and the Le Beef burgers drawing regulars. Those with more time can treat lunch like an occasion, pairing oysters or other raw seafood with a properly made martini. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: This East Austin restaurant is a neighborhood go-to for seafood lovers. Launched by the duo behind burger pop-up Le Beef, chef Justin Huffman (an alum of Uchi and French brasserie Justine’s) and his wife Nicole Rossi bring raw bar finesse to the table with oysters, conservas, and crudos, alongside classic delicacies like Dungeness crab and the couple’s wagyu quarter-pounder, the Le Beef Burger.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: FoodHeads
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: At FoodHeads, sandwiches are the star, whether you order from the wide-ranging menu — the Lami, with grilled salami and mozzarella on toasted multigrain bread with spinach, tomato, and balsamic reduction, is a highlight — or build your own. Colorful decor and a sunny patio make it a welcoming choice for breakfast or lunch. | Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: This West Campus favorite combines relaxed vibes with the precision of a sandwich shop. Pulled pork and squash-mozzarella options headline a menu that always keeps things interesting outside of the usual turkey-and-cheese options. The shady patio provides a perfect spot to linger. Warning: Lunch gets pretty busy when the UT kids are in school, and the best way to avoid that trap is to place an order online.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin; Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Frazier's Long & Low
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: On Riverside, Frazier’s leans into the retro bar vibe with 1970s wood paneling, cold beer, and unfussy comfort food in a casual environment. It also serves a sneaky-good burger. The $6 Little (OG) comes classic-style with a smashed patty, shredded lettuce, and special sauce. Patty melts, chopped cheeses, and there are also vegan burgers made with Beyond Meat. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: On East Riverside, Frazier’s offers diner fare with a dive bar personality. It opens at noon daily, serving up smash burgers, chili cheese dogs, and cold beer with a side of ’90s nostalgia. The backyard patio is roomy and relaxed, ideal for casual meetups or playing hooky from work. It’s unapologetically laid-back.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Garrison
address: 101 Red River St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Nestled into the bottom of Downtown’s Fairmont Austin, this Michelin-recommended high-end hotel restaurant features a bunch of steaks in its serene dining room. From the strip loin to the giant 24-ounce porterhouse served with ramp butter, all meats are cooked in the post-oak-fired grill. While there’s no set dress code, per se, people do tend to spiff up for a special occasion. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Though the windowless ambiance at this restaurant within the downtown Fairmont hotel may not be to everyone’s taste, the kitchen is doing everything right. The menu rotates frequently and ranges from super-fancy tater tots with black truffle and aerated gruyere to caviar with brioche to large steaks. Expect five-star fine dining service as well. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Hestia
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Executive Chef Kevin Fink and partner Tavel Bristol-Joseph (both from Emmer & Rye, Canje, and Ezov) oversee this Downtown grill house, which is aptly named for the “Greek goddess of the hearth.” Every dish at this one-Michelin-starred restaurant is touched by its impressive 20-foot hearth, which uses a fusion of fire techniques from around the world. This includes its Texas wagyu sirloin and rib-eye steaks. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Named after the Greek goddess of the hearth, this Michelin-starred downtown restaurant — which comes from the much-lauded team behind Emmer & Rye — centers on a live-fire menu featuring dishes like dry-aged wagyu rib-eye steaks, oyster mushrooms, and chicken with peach lacquer and pickled muscadine grapes. Desserts come from Tavel Bristol-Joseph.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Hole In The Wall
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This unpretentious dive bar and live music venue has been serving cheap drinks for over 50 years. Located on the Drag, it’s a popular spot among University of Texas students and punk and rock music fans. It features three bars, two stages (one for karaoke), and a small outdoor patio. The mix of local and touring acts and the casual atmosphere make it a favorite among music lovers and those who just love to drink. | Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: A legendary dive bar known for being the humble beginnings of big local acts like Spoon, Black Joe Lewis, and Shakey Graves, chances are decent that a new band you see on stage in the front window of the Hole in the Wall will go on to be legends. Find a spot in the alleyway for a break from the dive bar inside and check out the backside business, Amity’s Coffee and Karaoke.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin; Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Home Slice
address: 1415 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Follow the fragrance of garlic to find this pizzeria dishing out New York-style pies and more. The giant, stuffed-to-the-brim Italian sub here has a dedicated fan club. If you can’t grab a table indoors or outdoors at the restaurant proper, order online or duck next door to More Home Slice for pizza to-go. | Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Travis Heights’ Home Slice isn’t just about pizza. Subs like the meatball, chicken, and eggplant parmesan, along with one of the best Italian subs in town, round out a strong sandwich lineup. Pro tip: The sandwiches are huge, so get a half unless you want leftovers, and add cherry peppers for a little extra kick. The North Loop locale offers the same signature subs.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin; Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Intero
address: 2612 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, Texas 78702
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Chef and co-owner Ian Thurwachter uses beautiful fresh produce from local farms and whole-animal cooking to create memorable Italian dishes like roasted duck breast over risotto, springy pastas like mafaldine with rosemary-tinged Bolognese, and pizzas at this restaurant on East Cesar Chavez. The menu changes constantly, but the pastas and anything with duck are great bets. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: The Holly restaurant is ever-so-considerate about its comforting Italian food, from hearty large meats like wagyu beef shoulder or full pasta plates such as the ricotta gnocchi with duck confit. Everything is thoughtfully sourced and prepared. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Joe’s Bakery & Coffee Shop
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Founded in 1962 by Joe and Paula Avila, this historic Mexican bakery is a pillar of the East Austin community, now run by the Avilas’s granddaughter Regina Estrada. Diners will first encounter the exceptional daytime menu, with wonderfully tender and juicy barbacoa breakfast, excellent migas, filling lunch tacos like chicken and beef picadillo tacos served with diner’s choice of flour, corn, or crispy shelled tortil | The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: If a condo displaces Joe’s, the Austin we know will end. Although Joe’s may seem unassuming, the daytime Holly Tex-Mex institution has racked up recognition from the James Beard Awards and Michelin. Joe’s typically incurs waits and lines, with fancy folks jammed up against those catching a quick meal between construction gigs.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Komé
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Co-owners Také and Kayo Asazu offer their takes on homestyle Japanese fare at this laid-back North Loop restaurant that offers excellent quality at a reasonable price. The menu offers a little bit of everything: starters like brie tempura, creative rolls like the sunshine roll with salmon, mango, and avocado, and luxe cuts like otoro nigiri, when available. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: The lunch menu at the North Loop Japanese restaurant is somewhat different from its dinner offerings. There are teishoku (lunch combinations) with mains like karaage, katsu, and hangar steak, we well as sushi lunch specials and rolls, bento boxes, and donburi and chirashi bowls. It’s available from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. on weekdays and until 3 p.m. on weekends. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Lutie’s
address: 4100 Red River St, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Reservations are essential at this tiny garden-forward restaurant at the dreamy Commodore Perry Estate. The New American restaurant from lauded Austin chef couple Bradley Nicholson and Susana Querejazu, has familiar regional dishes like Texas beef with marrow and sweet potato, while the chickpea and green corn salad and kouign-amann ice cream remain signature items. | Austin Restaurants With Great Views: The fancy, verdant New American restaurant within the Commodore Perry Estate is beautiful inside and out. The patio and many tables in the dining room overlook the estate’s beautifully manicured grounds. Plus, the restaurant earns high marks for its dishes, such as the fresh raw vegetables in the petit aioli or the buttery kouign amann ice cream.
article_titles: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants; Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Matt's El Rancho
address: 2613 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Dip into Austin history at this classic South Lamar Tex-Mex restaurant, where the margaritas are plentiful and the iconic Bob Armstrong dip is the ideal queso. Here, you can find such big-plate fare as the “deluxe” dinner with chile con queso, a beef taco, pork tamale, smothered enchilada, rice, and refried beans, all served in a vintage-feeling dining room. | The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The appeal of this historic South Lamar restaurant lies in its menu of Tex-Mex specialties and the massive patio centered around a fountain. Its size makes it a good spot for groups and family outings—there’s room for 500 people. Though the tostadas compuestas and the beef enchilada are stellar, everyone’s really there for the Bob Armstrong dip and a Mexican martini.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin; The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Mercado Sin Nombre
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Off Pleasant Valley in East Austin, this coffee shop brews an excellent cup of joe that’s light on astringency and heavy on complex flavors. The signature Atole Cortado pairs espresso with masa-infused oat milk and a side of atole, a warm masa-based drink flavored with cinnamon and vanilla. | The Best Coffee in Austin: The beloved farmers market stand expanded with this lovely alleyway coffee shop in the Holly neighborhood. The menu is short but sweet, making use of its Mexico-sourced beans in standard coffee drinks as well as special ones. Look no further than the atole cortado — aka the masa milk shorty — where espresso paired with a candied orange slice is accompanied by a sidebar of the masa beverage.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Mozart's Coffee
address: 3825 Lake Austin Blvd., Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The spacious Lake Austin cafe wins for beautiful scenery (Water! Birds! Fish!). Though the coffee, Italian sodas, and baked goods take second place to the view, they’re really quite good. There are indoor areas with big windows, but why not get right next to the water? | Austin Restaurants With Great Views: This West Austin coffee shop’s expansive tree-shaded patio is right on the lake, making it perfect for leisurely drinks and snacks. Many sober dates have taken place here over pretty cakes and Italian sodas. In addition to the water views and the trees of Red Bud Isle across the lake, Mozart’s often hosts events or live music, making for a picture-perfect outing.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Musashino Sushi Dokoro
address: 2905 San Gabriel St Ste 200, Austin, TX, 78705, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: West Campus is the perfect setting for this textbook-traditional Tokyo-style sushi. Open since 1993, Musashino has become an Austin icon, and many of the city’s sushi greats trained here, including Uchi’s Tyson Cole, Také Asazu of Kome and Uroko, and Fukumoto’s Kazu Fukumoto. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Plenty of Austin’s best sushi chefs have cut their teeth at this longtime Japanese staple, which relocated just to the north of West Campus in 2016. It still offers some of the city’s best nigiri. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Nixta Taqueria
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Chef Edgar Rico has to be one of the country’s most lauded taco chefs, winning the James Beard Awards’ emerging chef of 2022 and Michelin Guide’s Texas Young Chef Award. Rico works magic into every single one of Nixta’s tacos and tostadas, pulling influences from fine dining, Mexican-American cuisine, the on-site garden, and his wife and co-owner Sara Mardanbigi’s Iranian background. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Nixta may feel like a garden party, but this counter-service taco restaurant in East Austin has racked up recognition from Eater Austin, the James Beard Awards, and Michelin. Chef Edgar Rico serves luxe tacos, like duck carnitas, plus dishes that call on his wife and co-owner Sara Mardanbigi’s Iranian heritage, like rice custard with cardamom, turmeric, pistachios, and cinnamon.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Oddwood Brewing
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ This lively MLK brewery fires up fantastic personal-sized, 10-inch thin-crust pies with loads of toppings, including meats (anchovies to Italian sausages), vegetables (broccoli to olives), and cheeses (vegan to ricotta). | The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: While the VHS tapes and indie rock soundtrack lend the MLK brewery a laid-back, retro vibe, Oddwood’s beers are smart and serious. The brewers clearly enjoy offering some sours, but most weeks, the tap list is heavy on IPAs and hoppier lagers. An extensive range of pizzas, salads, and stromboli help patrons soak in all of those beers, as does the stellar Egyptian barbecue food truck KG BBQ.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin; The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Olamaie
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This fancy Downtown Southern restaurant received a Michelin star for its sensational dining experience. Top Chef contestant chef de cuisine Amanda Turner and executive chef Michael Fojtasek collaborate on inventive plates, such as smoked cabbage and the blackened dayboat fish with blue crab sauce and Carolina Gold rice. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Executive chef Michael Fojtase’s Olamaie is another Austin favorite to claim a Michelin star. The modern downtown restaurant reimagines Southern classics – smoked cabbage with an Alabama barbecue sauce, blackened dayboat fish served with a blue-crab sauce, Carolina Gold rice, and chives, and grilled pork chops. Chef de cuisine Amanda Turner’s weekly six-course dinners are still a must-book for $150.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Oria
address: 1530 Barton Springs Road, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Oria has transformed the Zoe Tong space on Barton Springs Boulevard into a Mediterranean wonderland. The vibe is neutral colors with wood, stucco, and greenery everywhere and a wood-burning oven where diners can watch pita bread cooking while they sit with a fig leaf martini in hand. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Oria’s breezy patio feels like a trip to the Mediterranean with a neutral palette, plants everywhere, and gauzy curtains; it’s the patio of choice for anyone looking for the perfect photo op. The food is pretty typical Mediterranean fare — mezze, whole roasted branzino, and sorbets — but pita from the wood-fired oven is a fun addition.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
opening_hours: Sunday-Thursday 4pm-10pm Friday-Saturday 4pm-11pm Contact (512) 220-0204 contact@oriarestaurants.com; Sunday-Thursday 4pm-10pm; Friday-Saturday 4pm-11pm

name: Otoko
address: 1603 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Still one of Austin’s most exclusive dinners, Kyoto-meets-Tokyo in this multi-course journey led by chefs Yoshi Okai and Domonic Candy. The 12-seat Japanese restaurant, located at the South Congress Hotel in the Travis Heights neighborhood, offers two options: sushi omakase for $250 per person and classic omakase for $200 per person, making it a bucket-list experience. | Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Hidden upstairs in the South Congress Hotel, Otoko offers a moody, minimalist dining experience centered around an intimate 12-seat omakase, led by chef Yoshi Okai. The menu blends Tokyo-style sushi with Kyoto-style kaiseki, shifting nightly based on seasonality and Okai’s whims. Expect standout bites, such as toro with caviar or house-aged mackerel.
article_titles: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants; Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The Eater Award-winning casual counter-service North Loop restaurant is a premier destination for comforting Thai Chinese dishes courtesy of chef Thai Changthong. The namesake khao man gai is the Thai iteration of Hainanese chicken, with tender chicken paired with chicken-fat rice, a sweet chili sauce, cucumbers, and chicken broth. Don’t skip the noodle soups or the daily specials. | 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: In North Loop, P Thai’s began as a small food stall before evolving into a counter service restaurant centered on khao man gai, the Thai-Chinese chicken and rice dish served with soup. Diners can choose poached, fried, or a combination of both (recommended), each paired with fragrant chicken-fat rice and crisp cucumber.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
hours_match_name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]

name: Palm Pizza
address: 1701 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ The folks at Palm not only make their own dough, they also make their own mozzarella cheese — and, boy, does it make a difference. This charming green-accented pizza joint on E. Cesar Chavez, run by the folks behind Bufalina and Bufalina Due, knows how to balance the flavors on its NY-style pies. | The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: Neapolitan pizzeria Bufalina attracts a lot of attention, but New York-style offshoot Palm Pizza has truly excellent pies. Though it doesn’t have the best ambiance — seating is all outdoors and overlooks a parking lot — the pies are incredible. The Palm is a favorite, amped up with melty stracciatella, and thick-crust square slices available in pepperoni or cheese that are a must.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin; The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Paprika ATX
address: 6539 North Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78752, United States
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This beloved trailer expanded to a restaurant (with air conditioning) in 2025, and taco lovers rejoiced. Paprika exudes feel-good vibes in its compact new space, serving classic tacos like carnitas, tinga de pollo, nopalitos, and champiñones, along with tortas, quesadillas, and specials. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: The brick-and-mortar version of Paprika ATX recently opened near its original food truck location in the Highland neighborhood, and it’s already drawing long lunchtime lines that, thankfully, move quickly. The interior is sparse, but strong air conditioning and an upbeat crew make waiting easier. Suadero is a must-order, rich and deeply flavorful, while quesitacos pile on extra cheese for a yet more indulgent bite.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Posse East
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: Typically, the oldest bars end up being the diviest and Posse East is no exception. Since 1971, the bar has been slinging beer and wine — none of the hard stuff — to generations of UT students and colorful Austinites. The long-running institution has everything you could ask for, like worn-in booths, fatty burgers, and wild nights. Game days, when the place is a sea of burnt orange, are even wilder. | Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: At Posse East, the action centers on game days, when crowds pack the patio and dining room for wall-to-wall sports coverage. Football fans can nosh on burgers, fries, and, of course, queso. Cold pitchers, strong drinks, and wallet-friendly prices fuel the lively atmosphere.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin; Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Radio Coffee & Beer
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The South Lamar coffee shop and bar is popular for its triple threat of excellent coffee, great food trucks, and casual patio. Take your pick of lounging spots: the shaded outdoor space, the patio, or the grassy lawn. Good luck choosing between beloved taco truck Veracruz All Natural, Shortwave Diner, and Michelin-recognized Briscuits for food. | The Best Coffee in Austin: South Austin’s cafe and bar features good coffee, lots of outdoor space, and a whole lot of parking. For food, there are famed food trucks: Veracruz All Natural (tacos), Dee Dee (Thai), and Briscuits (barbecue and biscuits). In the evenings, it has booze too, and live music. It has a second location in East Austin on Montopolis Drive.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Restaurant Francois
address: 401 West 3rd Street, STE 100, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: French food is having a moment in Austin right now, and this restaurant from Guy + Larry Restaurants (Salty Saw, Red Ash) is going all-in on the bit. The downtown restaurant, which opened in December, serves dishes like Maine lobster thermidor, duck confit, boneless Wagyu short rib bourguignon, and the beefy dry-aged steaks the restaurant group is known for. | The Best Steakhouses in Austin: The team behind Red Ash Italia and J Carver’s Oyster Bar & Chophouse embraces joie de vivre at this Downtown newcomer while still maintaining a sharp steakhouse focus. The space is ideal for date night, with a sultry 1930s supper club feel that includes glowing globe lights, brass accents, and warm, low lighting. Large dry-aged rib-eyes and porterhouses are priced by the ounce and built for sharing.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin; The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Sam's Town Point
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: Way South Austinites don’t have to go far for a classic dive bar experience with live music, cold drinks, and a laid-back backyard vibe that’s away from the hustle and bustle of Downtown. This low-key bar is known for its chill atmosphere, friendly bartenders, and plenty of good tunes. There’s also a sprawling outdoor area with picnic tables. | Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Sam’s Town Point A bar time forgot, Sam’s Town Point is the place to go to when you’re missing the country days of Austin without someone trying to sell you on “authenticity.” Bite into a value-brand burger, sip a cheap beer, and catch a country act that will blow your boots off.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin; Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Soto South
address: 1100 South Lamar Boulevard, Suite 2115, Austin, Texas 78704
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Rolls at this Japanese restaurant can be spendy but high-quality, like the decadent salmon with shaved truffles. There’s also great nigiri for a more simple date night. There are two locations — one in South Lamar with a nice patio and the original in Cedar Park that’s indoor-only — and both accept takeout orders online. | The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: When Uchi is impossible to get into, or you’re looking for a different approach to Japanese cuisine, head to this restaurant in the Lamar Union development. The restaurant has well-crafted nigiri, sashimi, and sushi, alongside larger dishes like uni pasta and lobster tempura, as well as lunch specials. (The wine list is nice, too.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Sway
address: 3437 Bee Caves Rd, West Lake Hills, TX 78746, USA
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: In Westlake, the last-remaining Sway location offers a more polished take on Thai cuisine. The menu leans into creative interpretations, like spicy jungle curry and son-in-law eggs, served in a sleek dining room built for group dinners and celebrations. There’s also a great patio with views of Downtown Austin. | Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Austin’s only remaining location of the upscale Thai restaurant is a rare spot with food that is as good as the view. The covered upstairs patio offers a longshot view of Downtown Austin, which is the perfect backdrop for catching up with friends over drinks and plates of shu mai, panang short rib curry, or a consistently creative preparation of whole fish.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin; Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Tamale House East
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Looking for a lush, tranquil escape before heading out on East Sixth? The historic Tex-Mex restaurant on East Sixth offers plentiful outdoor space in its plant-filled courtyard. It’s known for tamales, sure, but there’s also excellent breakfast tacos, enchiladas, and margaritas. | The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The East Austin restaurant is part of Austin’s folkloric Tex-Mex history for good reason. The family dishes out steadfast migas, enchiladas, tacos, and its namesake tamales (regulars can easily get it all to go online, too), as they have been for over 60 years. Raise a toast to the portrait of Frida Kahlo that hangs over the bar and enjoy the vintage decor from any of the restaurant’s haphazard tables or patio seats.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin; The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: The ABGB
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ The vibes at this indoor-outdoor South Austin venue/brewery/pizza joint/hangout spot are great: a little rock n’ roll and also welcoming to anybody and everybody. The beer and the food are just as good. Pair a Hell Yes Helles with a wagyu beef-topped pie, or an Industry Pilsner with a Malabar Spinach slice, which has a pecan pesto sauce, some briny veggies, and a lemon herb yogurt. | The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The Galindo brewery has a lot of accolades, including winning multiple Great American Beer Festival golds (including the best large brewpub of the year), all done by keeping a laser focus on the oft-underrated pilsner and helles styles.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin; The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: The Continental Club
address: 1315 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Inside one of Austin’s most legendary venues in operation since 1955, get transported back to the rockabilly era with classic rock, blues, soul, and more every night of the week. Keep it simple at this Travis Heights spot with a brew or a vodka soda. After the show, plan on walking a block north for a slice of pizza from Home Slice’s late-night takeaway window, More Home Slice. | Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Open since 1955, the Continental Club hosts live music nightly on the stage that Stevie Ray Vaughan, the Replacements, and Robert Plant have all played on. The no-frills dive bar is the perfect spot to grab a cheap beer and reminisce about how Austin used to be. Check the schedule, as shows may be sold out and usually require a cover or ticket to enter.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food; Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: The Silver Medal
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Perched off the 290 frontage road in Coronado Hills, Silver Medal is a genuine dive bar that embraces its unpretentious charm. Inside, find wood-paneled walls, a jukebox, and a menu featuring standout smash burgers, chili dogs, and a ton of vegetarian-friendly options. The bar offers $2 Jello shots, beer on tap, and a laid back atmosphere that feels like a friend’s basement hangout. | The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This Coronado Hills bar is laidback with cheap drinks, simple food (the burger is excellent), and a resident cat named Orange Cat, who isn’t allowed inside but still sneaks in. The exterior may be a bit dingy, but that’s to be expected from a dive bar located in a motel parking lot. Check out Twofer Dog Night on Thursday, or take advantage of the $12 special, which includes a chili dog, burger, and a Bud.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas; The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: The White Horse
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This lively dive bar and honky-tonk in East Austin is known for its nightly live country music and spacious dance floor. The bar offers cheap whiskey and attracts a mix of old-school two-steppers and young hipsters, embodying both classic and modern Austin charm. Those looking for a break from dancing can enjoy pool tables and a relaxed outdoor patio with the Bomb Tacos food truck. | Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: The White Horse is an oasis of two-stepping, cheap beer, and Bomb Tacos in a sea of overpriced cocktail “experience.,” This bar comes to the rescue for all modern-day lovers of old-school country. Live music on the stage inside can be expected every night, and on Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays at 7 p.m., country-curious folks can learn two steps from the bar’s in-house instructor, Beth Coffey of Dancin’ Austin.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin; Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Toshokan
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: The East Austin Japanese restaurant has to be the only Michelin-recommended sushi spot operating within a mini-golf course (don’t worry, after passing through the secret door, the interior reflects the high-end omakase experience this is). Chef Saine Wong treats every omakase seating at the sushi speakeasy-like a dinner party with friends, occasionally pulling down his guitar for a serenade. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: The Japanese restaurant is hidden within East Austin’s Holey Moley venue, making it one of the most fun and memorable higher-end omakase experiences around. Led by chef Saine Wong, the $160 14-course meal showcases his global culinary experiences in delicious dishes. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Tsuke Edomae
address: 4600 Mueller Blvd Apt 1035, Austin, TX, 78723, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Chef Michael Che is behind one of the best immersive omakase experiences in Austin, hands down. Reservations for this tiny eight-seat indoor sushi bar in Mueller are essential and must be made the second bookings become available — unless there’s a rare opening in the waitlist. The meals focus on edomae-style sushi, which is largely unadorned, so there’s little flash and flair — just excellent sushi. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: One of the city’s hottest omakases that is highly difficult to get to tickets for is this Mueller Japanese restaurant led by chef Michael Che. If you’re one of the lucky ones, you’ll experience a thoughtful 21-course sushi journey for $135 with such top-notch quality ingredients. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Tzintzuntzan
address: 2302 West North Loop Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78756, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: An absolutely stunning creation from the team behind nearby Mexican icon Fonda San Miguel, Tzintzuntzan (which means “place of hummingbirds” in the Purépecha language) finally opened in February as a fancy breakfast and lunch spot. Dishes include enfrijoladas, chilaquiles with an earthy red or piquant green salsa, and decadent chocoflan. | The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: If brunch in a sculpture garden is the goal, Tzintzuntzan is the spot for you. The patio at this breakfast and lunch spot — sibling to nearby Fonda San Miguel — features beautiful sculptures, a cactus garden, and a relaxing fountain, and the interior is equally lovely. Grab some mole poblano biscuits, a cocktail, or velvety coffee and enjoy a soothing escape from the real world.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin; The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Uchi & Uchiko
address: 801 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Uchi and Uchiko are classic Austin Japanese restaurants for good reason. Both have some of the best, most inventive sushi in the city. Prepare to spend money or opt for the more budget-friendly sake social hour. Takeout orders can be placed online (Uchi, Uchiko); there are DoorDash deliveries (Uchi, Uchiko); and there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services. | The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: Still iconic. The Zilker restaurant’s modern take on Japanese cuisine has rightly earned its national acclaim. While you can’t go wrong with the exquisite à la carte menu, choose the adventurous market-priced omakase menu to truly experience the chef’s expertise with fresh cuts of fish, inventive flavor pairings, and desserts that surprise. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Uroko
address: 1023 Springdale Rd, Bldg 1, Suite C, Austin, TX, 78721, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: The second restaurant from the Kome team is a stall in the Springdale General complex that’s been freshly anointed by the James Beard Awards. By day, it offers to-go hand rolls (there are indoor and outdoor dining areas if staying on-site), and by night, a 45-minute power omakase at the sushi counter with all the classics. There’s also an at-home omakase offering, and reservations are required for either omakase. | The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: East MLK’s Uroko, inside the Springdale General complex, takes a minimal approach to maximal flavor. Build a hand roll lunch with options like avocado with yuzu hama toro, spicy tuna, and beef tataki. The sleek, counter-style space is calm and efficient, well-suited to a quiet midday break. Lunch is served Monday through Saturday from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. and until 3 p.m. on Sundays.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin; The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Walton's Fancy & Staple
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: A longtime player in the Warehouse District, Walton’s anchors a corner of West Sixth with old-school deli charm. Flaky quiches, well-stacked turkey sandwiches, and an eye-catching pastry case anchor a menu that consistently satisfies. Inside, the antique décor and floral touches make it feel timeless, in the best way. Open daily from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. | Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: West Sixth Street’s gorgeous deli-slash-florist strikes a balance between tradition and bakery charm. The Turkey Sweet sandwich layers bacon, white cheddar, caramelized onions, and apple chutney on a buttery croissant. Sandwiches here feel a little dressed up without going over the top. It’s a solid lunch spot with a pastry counter worth browsing. Get the sandwich you want quickly by ordering online.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin; Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: 29th Street Ballroom
address: 2906 Fruth Street, Austin, Texas 78705, United States
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: With a light renovative touch to the beloved Ballroom, the managers behind Tweedy’s, Hotel Vegas, Kinda Tropical, Chess Club, The Little Darlin’, and Yellow Jacket Social Club joined forces for a live music bar revival. Giving our city a mid-sized stage for mid-sized touring bands to perform on, 29th Street Ballroom fills a missing middle aspect to our venues, opening the door up to more kinds of musical acts.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: ALC Steaks
address: 1205 N Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Don’t be fooled by the baby strip mall location. This restaurant has been grilling steaks with a Southwestern twist for over 30 years. Though the name might’ve changed (it was formerly known as Austin Land & Cattle Co.), the casual vibes sure haven’t. Pass by the guitars on the walls and stroll across the saltillo tile floors to the dining room, where meats come to the table in all shapes and sizes.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Allday Pizza
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Since opening its two locations in Tarrytown and Hyde Park in 2023, Allday has established itself as one of the best spots in Austin for a big, perfectly foldable NY slice with a flavorful, chewy crust and some great specialty topping combos.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Allday Pizza [Tarrytown]
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This summer, Allday closed its East Sixth trailer behind Daydreamer to focus on its two brick-and-mortar locations in Tarrytown and Hyde Park — and the move seems to be for good measure. In an era when everyone’s charging what seems like too much for a slice, Allday delivers and is worth every penny.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Amaya's Taco Village
address: 5804 N Interstate 35 Frontage Rd, Austin, TX 78751, USA
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The Tex-Mex restaurant with two locations (this one in North Loop and the other down in Southpark Meadows) knows tacos, serving sturdy tortillas filled with eggs, fish, chicken, beef, or even rib meat. The dining room is a great place for a party, with twinkle lights and vibrant decor alongside tables that easily push together. So is the little outdoor patio, for that matter. And it does offer takeout online.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Amy’s Ice Creams
address: 3100 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
must_try: The Mexican vanilla ice cream that started it all.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: An Austin institution for more than 40 years, Amy’s serves thick, creamy ice cream out of 14 fun-loving shops in the area (including one that beckons locals and travelers touching down at Austin-Bergstrom International Airport). Amy’s is an endearing scoop of the oft-mourned “Old Austin,” its shops decorated with weird creations from employees, some of whom perform tricks as they serve.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Aris
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Houston-based restaurant group and James Beard Award semifinalist Sof Hospitality opened its fifth restaurant in Clarksville, bringing a slice of the Mediterranean to traditional Texas steakhouses. Aris’s dry-aging techniques are on full display thanks to a glass case located directly next to the dimly lit curved banquettes.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Arlo Grey
address: 111 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: In downtown Austin, the Line Austin Hotel’s restaurant — run by Top Chef winner and now host Kristen Kish — is a prime spot for watching the city’s famous bats. There are floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the Colorado River and the Congress Avenue Bridge. Kish’s menu includes creative smaller plates like crispy rice with crab and saffron aioli, and a great burger.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Arriba Abajo
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: One of Downtown’s better rooftop restaurants, Arriba Abajo, took over the Wax Myrtle’s space at the Thompson Hotel and is serving an Oaxacan menu that’s much better than a hotel restaurant needs to be. The desert plant-laden patio space contrasts with the tall buildings surrounding it for a beautiful view.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Artipasta
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: Opened by immigrants from the city of Mantova in the Lombardy region of Italy, this Italian food truck offers some of the most true-to-Italia dishes in Austin. Try pastas like the indulgently rich lasagne or casarecce with sage and almond pesto. There’s a second location up in the Linc that has an indoor dining area and a patio. Takeout orders can be placed online.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Asiana Indian Cuisine
address: 801 E William Cannon Dr, #205, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: This Far South Austin spot serves up some of the best not-to-be-missed South and North Indian dishes inside an unassuming strip mall parallel to I-35. From vegetable samosas, gobi manchurian (cauliflower florets cooked with soya sauce), to tandoori meats and sweet or salty lassis (a blended drink of yogurt, ice, water and spices), Asiana focuses on the vibrant flavors and hosts a simple dining space inside the resta
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Aster’s Ethiopian Restaurant
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Sop up all those wott sauces with spongy injera at this longtime Ethiopian Central Austin restaurant that’s perfect for carnivores and vegans alike. Takeout orders can be placed online; there are indoor dine-in services.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Asti Trattoria
address: 408 E 43rd St Ste C, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: This longtime Hyde Park restaurant attracts the all-too-rare intergenerational crowd with Italian favorites including pizza and hand-made pasta, plus “could’ve fooled me” gluten-free versions. For any-time-of-day pasta, try the creamy carbonara; brunch-goers should not miss the tart Italian toast, made on focaccia with ricotta and lemon curd, either.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Atlas Sandwich Company
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Inside the Armadillo Den in Far South Austin, Atlas Sandwich Company crafts Greek-inspired sandwiches that are packed with flavor. Lamb and chicken marinated in herbs get loaded with tzatziki, smashed potatoes, and fresh vegetables. The spicy whipped feta sauce belongs on everything. Grab a beer and enjoy it under the trees. Ordering online for pickup and delivery is available.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Austin Beerworks
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Perhaps the most successful manifestation of craft brewing in Austin, this North Burnet brewery has moved from strength to strength mostly by doing one big thing right: understanding what Central Texas beer drinkers want.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Austin Oyster Co.
address: 2500 E. Cesar Chavez St. Austin, TX 78723
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Transport yourself to a New England summer in this chic Holly neighborhood restaurant that opened in March. Even oyster naysayers will be converted by the impressive selection of briny treasures, curated by founder Brendan Yancy. The clams diablo — littleneck clams in a Calabrian broth with bloody mary relish — reportedly made the menu after approval from chef José Andrés.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Baldinucci Pizza Romana
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Come hungry to Baldinucci Pizza Romana, where the Baldinucci family, inspired by the Long Island pizzeria their father founded in 1977, bakes pies that are big, beautiful, and downright delicious. This Westlake spot makes pizza two ways, New York-style and Roman.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Banger's Sausage House & Beer Garden
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Dine on sausages and sip on draft beers while enjoying live music in this spacious Downtown beer garden on the weekends. While there’s plenty of space inside to find out why a sausage joint is always a hot dining spot, be sure to grab a table in the yard or on its porches to see bands put the banger in Banger’s Sausage House and Beer Garden.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Bar Toti
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Located next to its sister restaurant Este off Manor Road, Bar Toti serves a bold, spicy burger with serious flair. La Smashburgesa stacks two patties with white American cheese, escabeche, griddled ham, and a smoky chipotle mayo. It’s as indulgent as it sounds, best enjoyed with a 50/50 Manhattan (rye, amaro, rosso vermouth, Oloroso sherry, and mole bitters) on the sunny front patio.
