name: Barfly's
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: With its budget-friendly beers and well drinks served by genuinely friendly bartenders, this North Loop bar is a win for both the wallet and spirit. There are pool tables, a loud jukebox, and floorboards that have seen better days. No notes. Don’t trip on all those stairs, though. The ones in the back leading down to the patio are even more rickety than the ones leading up to the entrance.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Barley Swine
address: 6555 Burnet Rd Ste 400, Austin, TX, 78757, US
notes: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: This Michelin-starred Brentwood restaurant, one of Austinite chef Bryce Gilmore’s three, is where the team serves experimental New Texan dishes with a seasonal multi-course tasting menu for $115 that includes bites like pig face carnitas and sunflower and miso layer cake.
article_titles: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Barrett’s Coffee
address: 713 W Saint Johns Ave, Austin, TX, 78752, US
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The Crestview coffee shop and roastery serves up an array of espresso drinks, including a chocolate-orange blossom latte, iced coffees, matcha, and chai. This is the kind of shop one can park at for awhile, with a large, industrial indoor space and a few outdoor tables. Coffee bean and cold-brew growler deliveries can be ordered online but for those who want a cup to go, it must be gotten in person.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Barrett’s Too
address: 103 E North Lp Blvd, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Open from early until late, this vegan-friendly casual patio bar in North Loop is a great spot for extended hangouts. The bar serves cocktails and coffee (and doesn’t charge extra for nondairy milk) along with tacos and other casual fare. The patio has picnic tables, Adirondack surrounding fire pits, colorful murals, and a kid-friendly area for little ones to explore.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Batch Craft Beer & Kolaches
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The Mueller brewery/bakery/restaurant has proven to be wildly popular with both the neighborhood set and beer aficionados. While the kolache bakery is the understandable draw for some, there’s a very eclectic lineup of beers ranging from “PB&J kolache-inspired” fruit beer to a New Zealand black IPA.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Bella Donna Subs
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Southeast Austin’s Bella Donna Subs keeps it simple with cold cuts and strong flavors. The Flying Turkey comes with spicy mustard and avocado for a clean, bold bite. Sandwiches arrive fast and hold up well. Be sure to grab a cookie on your way out.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Billy’s On Burnet
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: This old-school spot in Allandale is the kind of place where the football games are loud, the beers are cheap, and the burgers are crave-worthy. The classic cheeseburger is all about simplicity: thick, juicy patty, melted American, toasted bun — no frills, no fuss, and it rings it at $14.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Black’s Barbecue Austin
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: At Black’s, barbecue lovers can get a taste of Lockhart without leaving Austin. Brisket, sausage, and ribs are smoked to perfection and served alongside classic, hearty sides that complement the meats. This location brings legendary Lockhart meats to a convenient spot near campus, making it a go-to for both students and locals. First-time visitors must try the standout sausage.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Blue Aspara
address: 1904 South Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: When it opened in 2025, this new Cambodian food truck immediately became a star on the Austin food scene. The menu includes fragrant dishes like fish amok (steamed fish in coconut curry) and the Nom Pahng sandwich (ground chicken or pork in a baguette with vegetables, topped with a heavenly coconut Sriracha sauce).
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Blue Owl Brewing
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: If you’re into sour beer, Blue Owl is the place you need to be. The brewery specializes in sour and tart-leaning beers poured right in the heart of East Cesar Chavez’s hustle and bustle. Grab a flight if you want to try a wide range of styles; seasonal releases are plentiful, and the Sour Cranberry Ale w/ Ginger & Oats is a fall and winter favorite.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Bob’s Steak & Chop House - Austin Downtown
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: This Dallas-based steak chain offers two locations: one in the heart of Downtown and one at the Omni Barton Creek Resort & Spa. Downtown is more refined, while the Barton Creek locale is more rustic, with historic flags adorning the walls.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Bombay Dhaba
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: The Bouldin Creek truck offers a nice array of north and south Indian specialties, such as dosas, idlis, fantastic samosas, and entrees like paneer tikka masala and spinach lamb. Takeout orders can be placed in person; there are outdoor dine-in areas.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Bombay Express
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: In far north Austin, this vegetarian and vegan restaurant serves up daily specials with both Northern — aloo (potato) parathas, puri bhaji puris paired with potatoes — and Southern regional fare, like many dosas. Inside, Bombay Express’s plain interior keeps the focus of each visit on the vibrant flavors coming out of the kitchen.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Bombay Street Food
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: With food trucks in both Anderson Mill and Cedar Park, Bombay Street Food is all about Indian snacks. From the crispy bursting pani puri (crispy hallowed balls filled with chutney, spices, potatoes, and chickpeas), the flavorful ragda chaat (potato patties topped with chutneys) and vada pavs (deep-fried potato sandwiches), to the samosa chaats (chunks of samosas topped with chickpeas and chutneys), the menu goes the
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Bombay to Kathmandu Kitchen & Curry Pizza
address: 6700 Middle Fiskville Rd Suits 406, Austin, TX 78752, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Now in Highland, Bombay to Kathmandu serves great Indian and Nepalese dishes. Momos, kormas, and thalis, plus some fusion like french fry chaat with chutney, yogurt, and chickpeas, can all be seen served around its dining room along with the signature dish: curry pizzas. Chicken makhani, paneer and chicken tikka masala, saag paneer, and lamb vindaloo all top crispy pie crusts for a spiced-up slice of pizza.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Boni’s
address: 1805 South 1st Street, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: The team behind South First staple Lenoir opened a tapas bar on the same property in March. Boni’s, named after co-owner Jessica Maher’s Spanish great-grandfather, is housed in the 1930s bungalow on-site, and serves cocktails (think gin and tonic goblets and seasonal margaritas), wine, espresso, and tapas like oysters, a ham plate, and head-on shrimp.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Broken Spoke
address: 3201 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Straight out of your favorite Western film, Broken Spoke is a real-life honky tonk bar on South Lamar, complete with a cast of local characters. Order something from the truly Tex meets Mex menu, like a quesadilla, barbecue plate, or chicken-fried steak, before gaining the courage to join the line dancing that takes over the dance floor.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Brown's Bar-B-Que
address: 1901 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: The Zilker barbecue truck at Corner Bar is known and loved for its ribs, brisket, chicken, and pulled pork. Don’t forget the sides: The homemade potato salad and coleslaw are worth attention. Diners can take their orders to go or sit at one of the truck’s adjacent picnic tables with their barbecue spreads and, if they know what’s good for them, a large sweet tea.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Buddy's Burger
address: 9001 Cameron Rd Ste 101, Austin, TX 78754, USA
must_try: The $5 hand-spun milkshakes include classic flavors like strawberry, chocolate, and vanilla, plus more inventive options, like Mexican vanilla and coffee.
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: This North Austin drive-thru hits all the right notes with crispy-edged smashburgers made from Angus beef and topped with fresh lettuce, tomato, and house sauce on a soft potato bun. Buddy’s is a family-run spot with quick service, indoor seating, and a few patio tables if you want to hang out. Burgers run from $5 to $8. Try the spicy version of the classic cheeseburger if you’re feeling bold.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Bufalina
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: For over a decade, Bufalina has been one of Austin’s go-tos for blistered Neapolitan pizza. It also slings fantastic house-made pastas such as pappardelle with ragu and orecchiette with sausage and preserved lemons. Sip a glass of natural wine from the extensive list outside while waiting (and on weekends, there will almost certainly be a wait) for a table inside to watch the wood-fire oven in action.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Bulevar
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: The Mexican restaurant is perched on a hill in the Arboretum area, so there are beautiful views of the rolling hills of West Austin from the enclosed patio and tables by the window in the dining room. The patio windows open up for an alfresco experience when it’s nice out. The gluten-free menu has charred carne asada, smoky duck enmoladas, and spicy salsas.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: C-Boy's Heart & Soul
address: 2008 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: This funky South Austin venue is the perfect place to start or end the night with live classic soul and R&B music every day of the week. Find space inside to watch the acts that come across its indoor stage, or catch a break from dancing on its patio out back. Currently, the Weird Food halal trailer is parked outside, dishing up Mediterranean dishes like falafel and gyros.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Cafe Malta
address: 3421 W William Cannon Dr, Austin, TX, 78745, US
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: Joining a proud tradition of exceptional Austin restaurants located in strip malls, Cafe Malta punches above its weight in terms of ambiance and creative European bistro fare. Expect seasonal dishes like fresh salads, coq au vin, and meaty brisket ravioli served with a pesto made of Texas pecans at surprisingly reasonable prices.
article_titles: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Carousel Lounge
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This Windsor Park circus-themed dive offers set-ups, beer, and wine. Open since 1963, it features an eclectic mix of live music, including rock and jazz sets, all of which are played beneath a giant pink elephant mural. It’s cash-only, like any good dive should be, but there’s no outdoor space.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: The Austin mini-chain offshoot of Perry’s, which has locations at Lantana Place and one up north in the Grove, is all about meat, which naturally includes steaks. Serving carefully chosen USDA Upper Choice or higher Midwestern beef, Carve’s steaks include bacon-wrapped, cherry-smoked filet mignon, 22-ounce bone-in rib-eye with caramelized onions, and butcher’s custom-cut steaks that are also available raw to take ho
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
opening_hours: Daily Happiest Hour Every day from 3 PM-6:30 PM; Monday through Friday from 11 am to 5 pm.

name: Casper Fermentables
address: 4715 S Lamar Blvd, Sunset Valley, TX, 78745, US
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: In Sunset Valley, Casper Fermentables serves sandwiches on house-baked sourdough and focaccia that deliver bold, fresh flavors. The Spicy Turkey features Sichuan chile oil, while the kimchi tuna melt balances funk and heat. The menu pairs creative sandwiches with a selection of house-fermented goods. Seasonal vegetables and pickles often add brightness and texture to the offerings.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Cenote
address: 1405 East 7th Street, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: A longtime East Side favorite has returned. After briefly closing in the spring of 2025, Cenote returned in September, this time with new digs on Seventh Street. The new location has all of the old favorites like chilaquiles, migas, breakfast sandwiches, and strong coffee, but also has a full bar this time around too.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Central District Brewing
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: For those attending Convention Center events or en route to a concert at Stubb’s, this Red River venue in downtown Austin is a simple, pleasant spot for a craft beer. Beers are cleverly named after song titles: on a recent visit, there were references to the Incubus and Florence & The Machine on the menu.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Central Machine Works
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Central Machine Works is a massive beer hall converted from a machine shop on the East Side, with tons of space both indoors and out. The brewery leans into pilsners, lagers, ales, and IPAs, plus cocktails like a paloma on draft, and a menu of pizzas, sandwiches, nachos, and pretzels.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Cheer Up Charlies
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Under the parachute tent or inside the bar, an evening spent at Cheer Up will be unforgettable — and no night here is the same. Expect the unexpected with DJ sets by drag queens, middle school-inspired dance parties, and musical acts covering every kind of sound. Asador, the taco truck out front, reliably serves both meat and veggie tacos with carnitas or cauliflower inside the tortillas.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Chicago Slice
address: 900 East Braker Lane, STE 105, Austin, Texas 78753, United States
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ If you’re a Midwesterner looking for true tavern-style pizza, North Austin’s Chicago Slice, which opened in May 2025, is here for you. A quick tavern-style primer: tangy sauce, bubbly browned cheese and any combo of toppings are all generously spread right up to the very edges of an ultra-thin crackery crust, then cut into compact, grid-style square slices (leaving four flavor-packed, bite-sized triangle c
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: China’s Family Restaurant
address: 1914 Guadalupe St suite b, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: On the Drag, China’s Family Restaurant offers some of Austin’s best Sichuan cuisine, with a menu that offers bold, traditional dishes. Standouts include mapo tofu, dan dan noodles, yuxiang shredded pork, and spicy eggplant with garlic sauce. The no-frills space keeps the focus squarely on the food.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Choo Sando
address: 5222 Burnet Rd Suite535, Austin, TX 78756, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Choo Sando crafts Japanese sandwiches that balance a sweet and savory flavor profile with a photogenic presentation. Fluffy milk bread holds together layers of pork katsu, egg salad, or seasonal fruit and cream. The shop began as a pop-up but quickly found a loyal following that goes well beyond its Rosedale following. The sandwiches are perfect for pick-up. Some sandos are available for pre-order 24 hours ahead.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Chuy’s
address: 1728 Barton Springs Rd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: Though it’s now a national chain, Chuy’s got its started at this colorful and funky restaurant on Barton Springs Road over 40 years ago. Along with the kitschy decor, expect serious Tex-Mex, tangy and creamy jalapeño dip, and some of the best fajitas in town (Chuy’s version is marinated with Texas treasure Shiner Bock beer).
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Ciccio Bomba
address: 1100 South Lamar Boulevard, STE 2140, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Comedian Tom Segura loved chef Gianba Vinzoni’s chocolate croissants at LA’s Cinque Terre West so much that, after moving to Austin, he partnered with the chef to open this bakery and cafe in his new hometown.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Cipollina
address: 1213 W Lynn St, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: This smallish, cozy restaurant in the heart of Clarksville focuses on Italian dishes with an Austin twist. For lunch, try the meatball sub on a crusty baguette. For dinner, go for a crispy thin-crust pizza, or try the Bolognese made with brisket, bacon, tomato, and tagliatelle, topped with a snowfall layer grana padano.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Clark’s Oyster Bar
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: It might be hard to believe that one of Austin’s best burgers is at this Clarksville oyster and seafood bar for $25, but so be it. Although the skinny shoestring fries are pretty divisive, it’s hard to argue with the thick, crusty beef patty doused in zesty gribiche sauce and Gruyere cheese. Order some bivalves for an appetizer and down your burger with an ice-cold, dry martini inside the sunny restaurant.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Clay Pit Contemprary Indian Cuisine
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Housed in a rumored-to-be-haunted building, this warm but upscale Indian restaurant offers both staples (like tikka masala, vindaloo, and goat curry) and new classics: Seek out the curried mussels, tandoori salmon, and rack of lamb infused with Kashmiri spices. At lunch, lighter curry bowls offer a quicker, more casual option.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Coconut Club
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: If any bar has good vibes in spades, it’s the outdoor rooftop dance party that is Coconut Club. Located two stories in the air in Downtown, under a giant neon red sign with the bar’s name, a never-ending rotation of DJs come through to deliver bouncing beats meant for dancing the night away. A vodka soda here just tastes better somehow.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Codependent Cocktails + Coffee
address: 301 West Ave STE 110, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Located at the base of the Independent Condo Tower in downtown Austin, this coffeehouse by day, drinks spot by night, boasts floor-to-ceiling windows that overlook Shoal Creek as it winds its way through the city. The verdant foliage, both inside and out, gives the space a treehouse-like effect from the interior or the patio.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Cosmic Coffee + Beer Garden
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: This sprawling South Austin space is lush with sustainable plants and water features. The cafe serves coffee and cocktails (try one of the frozens) and is home to great food trucks like Pueblo Viejo for excellent tacos. There’s a second location in East Austin close to downtown with a good patio (and great tacos) too.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Cosmic Saltillo
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: The popular South Austin coffee bar’s Eastern outpost finds a way to fit live music into its impressive lineup of coffee, cocktails, and tacos tucked inside a warehouse that will give anyone landscaping envy. Every Thursday, a stellar band sets up shop and serenades the two-story patio bar,s.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: County Line on the Hill
address: 6500 Bee Caves Rd, Austin, TX 78746, USA
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Located in a historic building that is an institution that has been around for 50 years, the West Hills Lake location of the Austin-born barbecue restaurant chain presents an incredible 20-mile view of the Texas Hill Country from its covered twinkle-lit stone patio. The barbecue is also fantastic, particularly the snappy sausage and peppery turkey breast.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Crown & Anchor Pub
address: 2911 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX, 78705, US
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: For almost 40 years, this casual North Campus pub has been slinging affordable bar fare like fried pickles, Frito pie, and some of the city’s best burgers to hungry college students and sports fans who can enjoy more than 30 beers on tap. The vibes here are immaculate, and walking in can feel like a time warp, as patrons shoot pool and chat with friendly bartenders, often with nary a phone in sight.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Dan's Hamburgers
address: 5602 N Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78751, USA
must_try: Make it a meal with one of its creamy malts.
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Dan’s has been keeping things simple since the 1970s, and that’s precisely the point. With four locations across town, including outposts in South Austin and Airport Boulevard, the focus remains on no-fuss burgers, fast service, and curly fries that always hit the mark. The spot is dependable, affordable, and proudly old school: score quarter-pound burgers for $5 and doubles for $8.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Dean’s Italian Steakhouse
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: White tablecloths and low lighting set a classic steakhouse mood in this polished Downtown dining room, which mixes Italian flair with a modern edge. The menu goes all in on beef, featuring everything from USDA Prime dry-aged cuts to Texas and Japanese A5 wagyu, alongside showstoppers like a 36-ounce tomahawk rib-eye and richly marbled strip loins.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Dee Dee
address: 1906 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: After closing in 2025, beloved Thai restaurant Dee Dee has made a triumphant return — with new menu items, to boot — as part of the absolutely gorgeous Leona space in Sunset Valley. Familiar dishes include om gai, a spicy chicken soup, and the tofu stir fry, plus new bites like dumplings and Thai fried chicken.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Deep Eddy Cabaret
address: 2315 Lake Austin Blvd, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: One of Austin’s oldest dive bars, Deep Eddy Cabaret, has been serving cold beer and good conversation since 1951. This no-frills spot near Deep Eddy Pool is known for its jukebox, neon signs, pool table, and laid-back atmosphere. Despite being in an area not known for dive bars, Deep Eddy Cabaret remains a classic hangout for those seeking old-school Austin charm. It has a rich history and a loyal crowd of regulars.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Delray Cafe
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: This East Side food trailer parked outside Nickel City is all about beefy, griddled sliders with a nod to Detroit. Don’t be fooled by the “slider” name — the burgers here are plenty big, especially if you go for the double or triple stack. Each cheesy burger is laced with mustard, grilled onions, and pickles, all of which are served on a steamed bun starting at about $7.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Desilicious cafe
address: 4101 W Parmer Ln Ste C, Austin, TX, 78727, US
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: The Milwood South Indian restaurant with an incredible punny name offers snacky vegetarian and vegan dishes. The cafe-like menu spans dosas savory and sweet (yes, there’s a Nutella one), along with various breads such as paratha and idli, bajji (fried vegetables and meats), and a really great drinks menu, including Indian filter coffee and masala chai.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Desnudo Coffee
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The East Austin coffee trailer has worked up a frenzy of interest in its coffee beans sourced directly from local farmers, which it uses in espresso drinks and nice lattes like the shoyu butterscotch or the brown sugar miso. There’s also a downtown cart location. It sells its beans online.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Dirty Bill's
address: 511 Rio Grande St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This place is not a misnomer – it’s a dingy but welcome respite amid West Sixth Street bachelor/bachelorette parties. The intimate Downtown bar, adorned with string lights, stickers, and a signature gnome, is a popular industry haunt due to its lively atmosphere and simple drinks.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Distant Relatives
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The McKinney food truck at Meanwhile Brewing has grown into an essential Austin barbecue spot — there’s a reason Eater named it one of the best restaurants in the country and in the city in 2021. James Beard Awards semifinalist pitmaster Damien Brockway and his team showcase the foodways of the African American diaspora.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: District Kitchen + Cocktails
address: 5900 W Slaughter Ln D, Austin, TX 78749, USA
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: South Austinites don’t have to trek North for luscious cuts of beef. Both locations of this New American restaurant serve up various steaks, including wagyu New York strips, rib-eyes, filet mignons, and massive tomahawk rib-eyes.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Donn's Depot
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: A dive bar in an old train station in Clarksville seems just about right. There’s live music and often free two-stepping dance lessons. It’s fantastically fun, and a photo shoot in the bathroom is a must. Even Dua Lipa visited when she was in town for ACL.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

Dosa Shack is all about dosas, from original styles with fillings such as potatoes and onions or cottage cheese, as well as uttapam, chaat, and sandwiches like its paneer spinach melt.

name: Draught House Pub & Brewery
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: With worn wood tables and hearty pints, this ultra-casual Rosedale brewpub wouldn’t feel out of place in the Shire. Grab a beer, cider, or draft cocktail to enjoy at one of many shaded outdoor tables, though there is an indoor area as well. On-site kitchen Little House serves pub fare like fish and chips, burgers, and sausages.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: East Side Pies
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ For a crispy, flatbread-like crust and smart, balanced topping combos, turn to any of East Side Pies’ three locations. Pies like the ricotta-sauced, bacon-and-mushroom-topped Moontower and the garlicky, pesto-sauced Olivia (with a pile of Mediterranean toppings including artichokes and three types of olives) are uniquely craveable.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: El Borrego de Oro
address: 3900 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: This casual Mexican restaurant is fairly nondescript inside and out, but the food is fantastic. Drop in for all-day breakfast, lunch, or dinner and join dedicated regulars for excellent enchiladas, crispy tacos, and caldos. The rich birria is also a must-try.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: El Patio Restaurant
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: This family-owned North Campus Mexican A North Campus staple since 1954, El Patio Restaurant serves a consistent Tex-Mex menu built on tradition. The no-frills dining room keeps the focus on hearty, satisfying meals — enchiladas and chalupas are the highlight — a formula that has brought diners back for generations.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

Taking its cues from its namesake neighborhood of Barcelona, the flavorful menu spans This tapas restaurant has a flavorful menu of small dishes (think pulpo a la brasa, pan con tomate, and cheese selections), crispy-bottomed paellas (including seafood, duck, and vegetable), and desserts like crema catalana (all available
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: EL TIGRE COFFEE
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: El Tigre Coffee serves up small-batch coffee from Brazil and Honduras in a vintage Airstream trailer. The operation is simple, with some tacos and pastries and benches out front, but this is perfect for an easygoing stroll on a brisk morning. Duck into Keep Looking next door, an indie newsstand with a curated selection of zines, international magazines, and vintage clothing.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Eldorado Cafe
address: 3300 W Anderson Ln #303, Austin, TX 78757, USA
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: This North Shoal Tex-Mex restaurant is full of old-school tastes and vibes thanks to its team and the owner, Joel Fried, who happens to be a former Tacodeli chef. There are cheesy enchiladas, piquant short ribs, and a spicy, flavorful array of salsas. The dining room features multi-colored walls, Home Depot tile floors, and a colorful stained glass feature over the open kitchen.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Electric Gravy
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Sisters with Chinese-American cuisine cooking neighbor Old Thousand, Electric Gravy is what would happen if a Mumbai bar suddenly found itself on East Eleventh. Before indulging in a curry like the Goan-style pork vindaloo with its spicy and tangy curry sauce, take a chip and dip it in the curry queso, made of tomato masala, tadka, and, of course, cheese, to experience the remix of expectations for flavors at a sit-
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Elephant Room
address: Swift Building, 315 Congress Ave., Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Duck into this basement jazz bar in Downtown to be transported back to the days when speakeasies were more than a trend. The Elephant Room is an effortlessly cool and cozy spot under a dress shop on Congress, with jazz bands filling the delightfully dingy space to play improvised tunes and/or classic jazz standards.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Enchiladas y Mas
address: 1911 W Anderson Ln, Austin, TX 78757, USA
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: There is one reason to go to this Crestview Tex-Mex restaurant, and it’s in the name: stunning enchiladas of all types, including enchiladas rancheras; enchiladas con queso; and beef, chicken, or vegetarian enchiladas. An order of chile con queso is never out of place on the table; the margaritas, served in a no-frills dining room, will knock you clear into next week.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Endo
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Endo, an unexpectedly great 10-seat omakase spot in West Campus, focuses on traditional edomae sushi. The traditional omakase is a 20-course menu that changes daily, but its $99 16-course menu is available on select dates and times. There’s also lounge seating to explore the restaurant’s substantial Japanese whisky collection.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Ensenada ATX
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This friendly orange truck in the Arbor Food Park in Central East Austin has a small but transformative menu. Ensenada serves Baja-style fresh seafood perfect for hot days: think crispy fish tacos and a cóctel de camarón topped with ripe avocado (the avocado is technically optional, but shouldn’t be).
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Épicerie
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Tucked into a quiet corner of Rosedale, Épicerie offers a relaxed midday retreat with a French Louisiana twist. The lunch menu here strikes a cozy balance between rustic and refined, with fried shrimp po’boys and refreshing Caesar salads. The side patio, shaded and cheerful, feels like a neighborhood secret. Lunch runs from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. daily.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Equipment Room
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: For fantastic high-end cocktails in a cool indoor space soundtracked by hip music, head straight to this stylish bar tucked inside the Hotel Magdalena. The bar offers classic cocktails like an espresso martini along with its “b-side” version, like Bottom of the River with gin, nori amaro, black sesame aperitivo, macadamia liqueur, and coffee. The bar also offers snacks like shrimp chips or a kobayashi dog.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Este
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Seafood-focused Mexican cooking sets Este apart in East Austin: Ceviche and shrimp aguachile deliver bright, layered flavors, while the crispy swordfish tacos are the epitome of crunch. (Although it’s not seafood, don’t skip the mushroom quesadilla.) The bungalow-style dining room provides a stylish but relaxed backdrop.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Fast Friends Beer Company
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Fast Friends has great beer and spacious indoor and outdoor areas with kid-friendly games. There’s a wide array of beer styles, like a hoppy East Coast IPL, a dark amber ale, and an easy drinking Czech pilsner. Wine, cider, seltzers, and nonalcoholic beverage options are also available.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Figure 8 Coffee Purveyors
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: This small Central East Austin coffee shop serves up its own roasted beans, along with other craft roasters. It sells shippable coffee beans, which patrons will find themselves standing next to bags of while waiting for their name to be called. It has a second location in North Loop.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Fixe Southern House
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: This upscale Southern restaurant is one of the better restaurant options Downtown, and it’s totally underappreciated. There’s a full bar, though the bourbon and whiskey cocktails get particular attention, like the Porch Swing with bourbon, peach liquor, lemon, tea, and spices.

name: Flitch Coffee
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The east side coffee shop is set up in a vintage trailer with plenty of outdoor seating. Beans come from local roasteries like Creature Coffee Co., among others, along with bites from Pueblo Viejo and Sour Duck.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Fonda San Miguel
address: 2330 W North Loop Blvd, Austin, TX, 78756, US
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: This high-end restaurant in Allandale is a classic for a reason: Since the 1970s, Austin co-founders Tom Gilliland and late chef Miguel Ravago are credited with focusing on fine-dining Mexican food whose origins can be traced to regions like Oaxaca, Puebla, Veracruz, and Yucatan. Classic regional Mexican dishes include an array of chile rellenos, cochinita pibil, and wagyu steak tacos.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Foodie + Tina
address: 2153 South Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: What began as a monthly pop-up omakase hosted in chefs Becca Seiler and Spencer Brown’s backyard barn will soon be a fully fledged brick-and-mortar. Set to open on South Lamar in the summer of 2026, the husband-and-wife duo will continue to build dinners around sustainably sourced produce and Gulf Coast fish (along with responsibly-caught options from the East Coast, West Coast, and Japan).
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Foreign & Domestic
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Chef-owners Sarah Heard and Nathan Lemley are still knocking it out of the park at this North Loop restaurant, which has been a neighborhood staple for 15 years — so much so that there would probably be local upheaval if any of its staple dishes were removed.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Franklin Barbecue
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: There’s a reason Franklin has sold out of brisket every day since opening in 2009. In a city known for smoked meats, Franklin stands out. Yes, the lines are long — but yes, the brisket is worth it, with a peppery bark that melts in your mouth.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Frazier’s Long & Low
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: Frazier’s has a couple of beers on draft and a small backbar of liquor. While the Pleasant Valley haunt’s only been open since 2019 — not quite old enough to have that worn-in dive bar patina — it still has the right vibe and the right prices.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Fresa’s South First
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: In the Bouldin Creek neighborhood, Fresa’s nails the lunch brief with two flavors of wood-grilled chicken (achiote and citrus and Yucatan spice), and serves a variety of vibrant bowls and tacos that travel well from plate to patio. The space feels like a mini vacation, with palm trees, bright tile, and breezy tables. Lunch is available daily from 11 a.m. to 10 p.m.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Owner Kazu Fukumoto is a Musashino alum, which means he pays close attention to the cuts of fish he uses for every single piece through the more casual East Austin restaurant. The yakitori, like the savory tamago or juicy chicken thigh, and specials are worth trying. The dining room is open and airy, so the kitchen is on full display, but takeout is an option, too.

name: Gibson Street Bar & JewBoy Sliders
address: 1109 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: With an ample patio, the Zilker bar is perfect for both intimate dates and large groups. The casual bar serves well-made classic cocktails, and numerous television screens make it a favorite for watching sports. Even better: there’s a location of El Paso-Jewish slider truck JewBoy here.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Gimme Burger
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Located in the courtyard at Butler Pitch & Putt, this Riverside Drive food truck is as much of a draw as the golf. The standard order is the namesake Gimme Burger, which features a double beef patty and double cheese for $12. The patties are smashed for a salty, edge-to-edge crust and come dressed with “shrettuce,” onion, tomatoes, pickles, and signature sauce on a sesame seed bun.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Golden Boy
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Chef Nick Middleton and the team behind Y Comida run this Fareground food hall stall on Congress, serving a tight menu built around bao, bowls, and salads. The lineup includes gochujang beef bao, miso butter salmon bowls, and a Buffalo chicken salad that leans indulgent without going overboard. Golden Boy keeps lunch-focused hours, open daily from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Golden Tiger
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: While the fried chicken sandwich at Golden Tiger is one of the main draws of this East Austin food truck, the double smashburger also stands out. Order a classic old fashioned or seasonal cocktail inside at Whisler’s and then head out back to the festive patio to chow down. The $12 burger comes with shredded lettuce, pickles, red onions, American cheese, and special sauce.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Goodall’s
address: 1900 Rio Grande St, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Located at the historic northern downtown Hotel Ella, which was built in 1900 and renovated in 2013, Goodall’s is the ultimate brunch or dinner spot with its elevated Southern fare and classy cocktails – ideal when the parents are in town. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Güero's Taco Bar
address: 1412 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Looking for tacos, margaritas, and music in the heart of South Congress? Hit up the lively patio at this Tex-Mex restaurant, place a large order for their chili con queso with Güero’s own ranchero sauce, and catch bands playing Thursday through Sunday. While the stage is out in its backyard, diners room can still hear the acts inside, albeit muffled.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Habana Austin
address: 2728 South Congress Avenue, TRLR 2, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Tucked beneath a canopy of tropical greenery, this nearly 25-year-old restaurant blends Cuban comfort food with a casual, breezy charm. The space includes a shady patio, thatch-roof cabanas, and plenty of cold mojitos. The lechon asado and ropa vieja keep regulars coming back, along with pressed Cubanos and garlicky yuca frita. Bonus: You can even order cigars to smoke in a cabana out back.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Handsome Dan’s
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Ready to explore a new sandwich shop? Handsome Dan’s is the bodega sandwich-loving Austinites need, and it opened in February on the Eastside. The cafeteria- and drug store counter-style seating is certainly retro. Josh Hare, the man behind Hops and Grain, has described Handsome Dan’s as a place designed to be just like “your high school football coach” — a stand-up spot that is dependable and delivers.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Himalaya Kosheli Nepali & Indian
must_try: Momos — the chewy, pan-fried Nepalese dumplings.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Austin’s South Asian food scene has a small but strong contingent of North Indian and Nepalese restaurants. Of those, this casual Barrington Oaks restaurant is a standout: It serves the greatest hits from the Indian subcontinent, including momos, chow mein, chilis, dals, Himalayan goat curry, and biryanis. Inside, find a cozy, diner-like interior with booths flanking the far wall and 1980s-style lighting.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Holiday
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: This Eater Award-winning Govalle bar has three outdoor areas, perfect for sipping fantastic cocktails like a frozen Mexican martini or one of co-owner Erin Ashford’s punch creations. With a certain kind of West Texas charm, Holiday is lovely and approachable, featuring white chairs and natural wood, cushy sofas, and misters for the super hot days.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: Home Slice [North Loop]
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Home Slice was the NY-style pizza place in Austin when it opened in the heart of South Congress in 2005. Plenty of tasty contenders have cropped up in the last two decades, but Home Slice’s pizzas remain big, savory and satisfying, with a nicely curated selection of specialty pies (the No.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Honey Moon Spirit Lounge
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: The elegant interiors at Honey Moon Spirit Lounge set a romantic tone for a night out in the Heritage neighborhood. The menu leans New American, with dishes like steak frites and seasonal desserts. A lively cocktail program makes it a strong contender for a celebratory dinner or date night, with drinks like the Yuzu Sexy (vodka, ginger, yuzu, lemon, lavender, and herb salt).
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Hopsquad Brewing Co.
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Major League Soccer fans flock to this North Burnet for the rowdy pregame atmosphere, which sits conveniently down the road from Q2 Stadium. The core beer lineup is available to-go in cans, and among the favorites are the Zapatista Mexican lager and (especially) the Architetto Ruffini Italian pils.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Hopsquad Brewing Co.
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Hopsquad sits just down the road from Q2 Stadium, making it an easy stop before or after an Austin FC match. The taproom pours a rotating lineup of IPAs, Mexican lagers, pilsners, and seasonal beers, plus the signature boozy Verde frozen lemonade — an ode to the FC. There’s also a tight slate of cocktails like margaritas, espresso martinis, and even THC options.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Hotel Vegas
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: On a strip known for bar hopping, consider staying the night for some shows at Hotel Vegas. With bars and stages inside and out, there’s a chance that if you don’t like what’s on for one stage, you’ll like what’s happening on the other.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: House of Three Gorges
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The casual North Austin Sichuan restaurant, which has vowed to “keep Austin spicy” since it opened in 2020, offers some of the best regional Chinese in the city. Find specialties like red braised pork belly, spicy jumping fish, and brisket in a tangy radish soup; the mapo tofu, silky chunks of tofu bedded in a savory sauce, is also a diner favorite.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Ichi-Umi Sushi
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: The North Austin sushi restaurant serves substantial sushi for lunch and dinner and is a popular choice for gluten-free and vegetarian diners. The slightly seared selection (aburi-style) is lovely. Dine in at the casual restaurant or order takeout online.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: III Forks
notes: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants: III Forks is a lavish Lavaca Street marble-and-mahogany-trimmed downtown steakhouse that leans into classic luxury with USDA Prime steaks, cold-water lobster, Oscar-style crab, black truffle-and honey-infused butter, handcrafted cocktails, and a wine list boasting over 500 selections.
article_titles: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants

name: Il Brutto
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: Only dine indoors at Il Brutto if you have to; the restaurant, set in the hubbub of East Sixth, boasts a large shady patio for people-watching plus occasional live music, the ideal backdrop for ultra fresh hand-made pastas. The lamb ragu pappardelle is a great order (even to-go), or snack on charcuterie and wood-fired pizzas over drinks. Don’t miss out on the rich desserts, like a ricotta semifreddo, before leaving.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Intero Restaurant
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: Both the dark indoor dining room and cute outdoor patio at this Holly restaurant offer great settings for a date night. Solo diners can also unwind over plates of pasta or hefty meat dishes at the long bar. Intero strives to avoid waste, utilizing every part of its ingredients wherever possible. The menu includes hand-made pasta like ricotta gnocchi with duck confit, plus lots of seasonal local vegetables.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: It’s Italian Cucina
address: 1500 S Lamar Blvd Ste 110, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Find handmade pastas and other Italian dishes from this under-the-radar Zilker neighborhood restaurant. The white-tablecloth spot with a grand piano (!) serves up Northern Italian pasta (try the gnocchi alla vodka), bruschette, risotto, and much more, all rounded out by a stellar wine list and original cocktails. Not feeling the dining room or patio? Order online for pickup or delivery.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Jaipur Palace
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Jaipur Palace is named after the capital of the state of Rajasthan, and serves a variety of regional dishes in Southpark Meadows. The menu highlights include tandoori gobi (cauliflower grilled in a tandoor), parathas and stuffed kulcha bread, biryanis, and a sweet and savory mango chicken dish.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Jester King Brewery
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The Hill Country brewery’s evolution from a beer nerd paradise to an all-around gathering spot appears to be complete. Once solely focused on farmhouse and wild beers, the brewery now offers several “clean” beer selections in the lager and pilsner categories, alongside curated picks of wines and ciders.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: JewBoy Burgers
must_try: Order the battered onion rings, but make them loaded by adding queso and chili.
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: JewBoy serves burgers in North Loop with a serious El Paso-slash-Jewish deli twist. The patties are pressed with grilled onions and layered with options like pastrami, green chiles, or even crispy latkes. It’s cozy inside with an extra few tables out front, and the vibe is laidback like the food, which is messy in a good way, and it’s affordable — the $11 burger is one of the cheapest items on the menu.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Josephine House
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Just off West Lynn in Clarksville, Josephine House is where weekday brunch doubles as an elegant lunch escape. The daily menu, available from 9 a.m. to 2:30 p.m., includes a carrot-ginger muffin, a burger layered with Gruyère and a farm egg, and more polished takes on daytime classics.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: June's
address: 1722 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Wine is the focus at this all-day cafe, with decor and a menu inspired by French sensibilities. Find dishes like French onion soup, a shaved jambon sandwich, and bone marrow bolognese. With a long marble bar, black and white tile, and outdoor tables, it’s fancy without being too fancy and a great option for a romantic dinner or catching up with friends.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Kai Zabb Thai
address: 7221 Mcneil Drive, Austin, Texas 78729, United States
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: Parked in a Highland food truck park, Kai Zabb Thai comes from the team behind hot chicken shop Dang Hot 89. The basil-green trailer specializes in fried chicken served over rice with sauces like fiery lime-and-chili or hearty panang curry, with choose-your-own spice levels. The chicken stays crisp and juicy, with dressings served on the side to lock in the crunch.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Kappo Kappo
address: 600 West 2nd Street, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Kappo Kappo brings a personal take on Japanese dining to downtown Austin. Opened by twin brothers Haru and Gohei Kishi, veterans of kitchens across Tokyo, Paris, and LA, the 25-seat restaurant focuses on kappo, a style that invites diners to engage directly with chefs at the counter as they prepare an 11-course tasting menu.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Kemuri Tatsu-ya
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Enjoy Eater Award-winning fare on the patio at this funky Japanese-Texas izakaya in the Holly neighborhood. Find unique dishes here like chili cheese takoyaki and a barbecue boat with serrano-miso barbecue sauce. The back area features plenty of covered and uncovered tables, and there’s a rambling indoor seating area, too, with walls covered in Japanese beer signs and deer heads.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: KG BBQ
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Austin veritably brims with barbecue restaurants, but this MLK Michelin Recommended food truck from pitmaster Kareem El-Ghayesh stands out because of the incredible Egyptian-Texan menu and minimal wait times. Look for pork ribs glazed with pomegranate seeds and za’atar, kebabs, turmeric rice studded with brisket and tahini, and smoked meats, available by the pound or in rice bowls and wraps.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Kick Butt Coffee
address: 5775 Airport Blvd Ste 725, Austin, TX, 78752, US
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: What happens when caffeine and karate combine? Kick Butt Coffee. This underrated spot continues to fly under the radar as it hosts open mics, live bands, and even comedy shows with the occasional barbecue in the parking lot.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: King Bee
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: A true East Side staple, King Bee swings wildly between energetic nights where folks are elbow-to-elbow chaos and the quiet, intimate ones, where there’s fewer than five people inside, making for a great intimate occasion with a dear friend or your thoughts. Very rarely is there a middle ground. The drinks are cheap, the bartenders swift, and the clientele typically awesome.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Kinsho
address: 51 Rainey Street, STE 140a, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: This sushi restaurant on Rainey Street opened in March from Nick Ford and Wade McElroy, the duo behind vibey North Loop Italian restaurant Rocco’s. Kinsho is not an omakase destination, instead it’s focused on a la carte nigiri, rolls, and skewers, with cocktails. The vibe is similar to Rocco’s, with dark booths and a cozy atmosphere tucked away from the noise of Rainey.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Knuckle Sandwich
address: 8504 South Congress Avenue, Austin, Texas 78745, United States
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Knuckle Sandwich brings bold East Coast deli energy to South Austin, offering a menu full of flavor. The signature Italian sub layers Olympia Provisions’ mortadella, capicola, and salami with Parm crisps, za’atar vinegar, and amba aioli on za’atar-dusted bread. Other standouts include the Not Boring Turkey Sammie, a lamb sandwich with tzatziki and beet horseradish slaw, and a mushroom Reuben made with lion’s mane.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Komé
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: This popular North Loop restaurant serves unique rolls, like the Texas Surf and Turf, an ode to the state made with shrimp tempura and hanger steak, maki, and hand-pressed sushi. The traditional offerings are stellar, as is the lunchtime chirashi bowl. There are a few dining tables outside, and expect a crowd pretty much all the time.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Konbini
address: 908 East 5th Street, STE 107, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Konbini, a late-night sushi spot collab between Papercut and Tare, began its pop-up in July. Chefs Michael Carranza and Danielle Martinez of Michelin-recognized Tare are running the kitchen, creating dishes that combine Japanese technique with Texan and coastal Mexican flavors. For now, it’s walk-in only. Head behind Papercut to find it.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Korea House
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Dining at the casual Korean restaurant in North Shoal Creek is a perfectly homey experience. The traditional menu boasts stews (budae jjigae and the seaweed-y miyukguk), kimbap, and kimchi jeon. Then, there’s the fun grill-it-yourself barbecue setup with all sorts of meats, such as galbi and tender duck breasts, and a wide variety of banchan.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Kyoko Coffee
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: This cute Japanese-inspired coffee truck (if one can call a Honda van a truck) describes itself as “a whole vibe,” and more than lives up to the promise. Some coffees have wacky names like Cause of Death and Kyoko Dreams of Ube. It also offers left-of-center options like Miso Pironcillo Umai and Umami Mocha, plus standard drinks like a cortado, cappuccino, and lattes.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: L’Oca d’Oro
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: This Mueller restaurant offers sustainable, locally sourced dishes like super-fresh mozzarella, sourdough focaccia, and an ever-rotating menu of pastas with both classic options (rigatoni alla ’nduja with spicy sausage) and inventive takes (fusilli with mustard cashew cream). If lasagna is on the menu, get it.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: La Barbecue
address: 2401 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, Texas 78702
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The city has an embarrassment of world-class smoky meats, and this East Austin spot has earned plenty of honors: a Michelin star, a spot on Texas Monthly’s best barbecue list, and a rave review from TikTok critic Keith Lee. The late LeAnn Mueller, of the legendary Texas barbecue family, launched the barbecue joint in 2012, and it’s now run solely by Mueller’s wife, Ali Clem.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: La Cocina de Consuelo
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: Consuela’s dream was to open a kitchen, and at the tender age of 63, she did in a converted house. Come to the Rosedale restaurant now run by her niece Letty Garcia for the amazing breakfast burrito bursting with whatever you want. The potato enchiladas with chile chipotle sauce are also great — orders for anything on the menu can be placed online.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: La Mancha Tex Mex Tavern
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: This casual Tex-Mex tavern in Rosedale offers dependable fare like queso, enchiladas, sopapillas, and more in a colorful setting. La Mancha is best known for its powerful margaritas, available in many fruity iterations like mango or sangria. The walls of the indoor dining room are covered with kitschy movie posters and light-up letters, and outside, there are several picnic tables for outdoor dining.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: La Plancha
address: 1701 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd Unit 101, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: This Blackland restaurant serves Mexican tortas with bold flavors and soft, warm bread. The menu includes brisket barbacoa, al pastor with grilled pineapple, Buffalo fried chicken, and a smoky burger-style torta. Breakfast features a chilaquiles torta, while a vegetarian option showcases a quinoa and black bean patty.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Lala's Little Nugget
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: It’s always Christmas time (as in it’s permanently decorated in festive Yuletide cheer) at this Crestview bar. Lala’s, which has been around since 1972, has lived many lives and was saved from an uncertain fate after an abrupt closure in 2015. Like any good dive bar, though, it has all the essentials.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Lamberts
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: In addition to a menu of slow-smoked meats, local beers, and classic cocktails, this longtime Downtown barbecue eatery from McGuire Moorman Lambert Hospitality group offers live music upstairs every weekend.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Lao’d Bar
must_try: Get as much of the menu as you can, but don’t skip the nam khao or the rib-eye skewer.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: The 2024 Eater Award-winning Far East Austin bar is chef Bob Somsith’s fully realized vision of fiery Laotian American cuisine, which he started during his food truck days. Lao’d Bar is all about full-on spices, from the nam khao (crispy fried rice with red curry) to its papaya salad, to the meaty skewers with dipping sauces (try the chicken with yellow curry and pineapple jicama slaw).
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Lazarus Brewing
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Pilsners and lagers dominate the list here, showing an appreciation for both Austin’s weather and the art of doing the basics right. There’s also a solid coffee program and a menu of tacos and tortas, making the space a vital all-day hang destination. Lazarus opened a second location on Airport Boulevard with 15 taps, wine and coffee options, a menu focusing on Mexican food, and a pirate ship play area for kids.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Lenoir
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The longtime favorite Bouldin Creek restaurant’s shady wine garden is an expansion of its vintage-chic interior. Another great spot for a nice date night, Lenoir is known for a great wine selection, unreal fluffy Pullman bread, and an ever-rotating menu of dishes made for hot weather. The wine garden serves the full menu, so try all of the Michelin-recommended fare.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: LeRoy & Lewis
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Garrison Park’s Michelin-starred LeRoy & Lewis rewrites the barbecue rules with bold choices like smoked beef cheeks, cauliflower burnt ends, and smoked burgers. Lunch runs Wednesday through Monday from 11 a.m. until sellout — and be warned: sellout comes quick. The flatiron, beef cheeks, and brisket specifically go fast, so showing up early pays off.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: LeRoy & Lewis [Restaurant]
must_try: Tender beef cheeks and that sinfully juicy burger.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Pitmaster Evan LeRoy, Sawyer Lewis, and their team made a name for themselves with this Michelin-starred, new-school barbecue restaurant in Garrison Park. Think intensely flavorful meats, including beef cheeks, smoked chicken, and pulled hog, plus dishes like chicken-fried steak specials and burgers. It may be a meat-centric spot, but don’t skip the vegetables, especially the cauliflower burnt ends.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Leroy And Lewis Barbecue
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: This celebrated, one-Michelin-starred barbecue joint in Garrison Park serves a burger worth seeking out. (It even received a shout-out on Somebody Feed Phil.) The juicy patty is a mix of brisket and beef, making for a smoky, decadent burger piled high with grilled onions, cheese, and sauce on a potato bun for $16. Have the burger with some sides or as a complement to a bigger group barbecue spread.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Lil Nonna’s
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ The name of this pizza food truck helps spell out a lot. Parked at Tweedy’s, Li’l Nonna’s aims to be that comforting spot you stop at for food that feels like a warm hug. What’s different, though, is Li’l Nonna’s offers its 14-inch New York-style pizzas and 12-inch gluten-free pizzas with various vegan and vegetarian toppings.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Little Deli & Pizzeria
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $ Considered one of Austin’s most underrated pizza spots, Little Deli and Pizzeria draws fans in from all over town for its Jersey-style pies and subs. With a second location in Windsor Park, the original Crestview restaurant offers a variety of pies each day, plus stromboli, soups, and sandwiches galore, including a diner-favorite Reuben and another stuffed with pastrami.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Little Longhorn Saloon
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: This Allandale bar has a storied reputation. On Sunday afternoons, Little Longhorn offers the classic Austin experience of Lone Star beers, live music, and a unique game called Chicken Shit bingo, where participants place bets on where a chicken will do its business. The drinks might not be fancy, but the music and dancing are always lively.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: Little Thailand
address: 4315 Caldwell Ln, Del Valle, TX 78617, USA
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: OK, yes, this Thai restaurant is technically in Del Valle, but the trek is well worth it for some of the greater Austin area’s absolute best Thai food. Signature dishes include red curry with roast duck and pad gra prow, a fragrant stir-fry of meat and vegetables. It’s family-owned and refreshingly no-frills.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Live Oak Brewing Company
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: What can you say about Austin’s oldest brewery, except that it’s a classic and the city is lucky to have it? Now almost 30 years old, the Del Valle brewery’s old-guard approach has always been to make German beer styles properly and priced reasonably. As such, both the Pilz and hefeweizen are among the best in the country.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Lonesome Dove Austin
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: The Austin outpost of Fort Worth’s 25-year-old Lonesome Dove Western Bistro brings Tim Love’s signature live-fire cooking to a more laid-back, semi-casual setting. There is still a nod to the restaurant’s game-heavy roots, but the focus here lands more squarely on steaks, with hand-cut options including beef tenderloin and a massive wagyu tomahawk ribeye.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Loro Austin
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: The Zilker pan-Asian-ish Texas smokehouse restaurant in the South Lamar neighborhood is a good crowd-pleasing destination — after all, it’s led by Uchi’s Tyson Cole and Franklin Barbecue’s Aaron Franklin. There’s brisket served with a chile gastrique (that’s restaurant-speak for a sweet-and-sour sauce), smoked turkey breast with yuzu chutney, and rice bowls alongside nice frozen cocktails.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Lucky Robot
address: 1303 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Chef Jay Huang focuses on sustainable and responsible sourcing at this South Congress Japanese restaurant. It’s popular for happy hour, where diners can snack on beef hot rock and sample dry-aged maki in the cherry blossom-adorned dining room or on the outside patio. There’s also online takeout for when pants are just not happening.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Madam Mam’s
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: The Thai mini-chain is a favorite amongst Austinites for its super-spicy fare. The menu, created by owner Chatfuang Mam, is a reflection of her food education, culinary knowledge, and generational methods. She was once married to Sap Apisaksari, but after the divorce, she kept the restaurant, and he opened Sap’s.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Magnolia Cafe
address: 1920 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: An Austin classic since 1979, this diner dishes out family-friendly breakfast and hangover-curing fare. The Mag Mud, a dip with black beans, queso, avocado, and pico de gallo, is a must. Though it’s no longer open 24 hours, diners have until midnight daily to grab those life-changing gingerbread pancakes in the dining room, on the patio, or to-go.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Maie Day
address: 1603 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: At the bottom of the South Congress Hotel, Maie Day feels equal parts casual steakhouse and celebratory dinner spot, with high ceilings, eclectic art, and party vibes. Chef Michael Fojtasek’s menu is playful and shareable, featuring options like juicy New York strip, crab claws, and sides like mac and cheese or loaded fries.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Marye’s Gourmet Pizza
address: 3663 Bee Caves Rd #4G, Austin, TX 78746, USA
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Pizza is just the starting point at this Westlake Hills pizzeria. Marye’s offers a full menu, featuring starters like meatballs, cheesy bread, and the lemon-feta hummus; sandwiches; salads; and rice and grain bowls.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Mattie's
address: 811 W Live Oak St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Remodeled from a historic South Austin farmhouse, this Southern fine dining restaurant in Bouldin Creek is an oasis right between South First and South Lamar. There are views of shaded oaks and grassy knolls as well as beautiful free-roaming peacocks, including a fetching all-white fowl. Mattie’s has excellent fried chicken and classic milk punch from a generational family recipe.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Maudie's Cafe
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The Austin chain reigns supreme for all things Tex-Mex in the city. The chile con queso is a solid pick, as well as the molten cheese-layered nachos, tender fajitas, and carne guisada tacos. Fair warning: The curvy walls in here might make you feel a little funny if you have too many margaritas. Let those flashing lights on the walls mesmerize, and then grab a breath of fresh air on the patio.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Meanwhile Brewing
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The brewery’s McKinney neighborhood aesthetic — industrial, lots of warehouses — gives drinkers no idea of what’s around the corner with its massive space. Upon arrival, there’s a fancy indoor taproom, a large stage, a soccer field, and perhaps the largest brewery patio area in the Austin city limits.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Mi Madre's
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The Cherrywood restaurant is a pillar for Tex-Mex in the neighborhood, offering massive burritos, sizzling chicken or steak fajitas, and perfect crispy tacos with picadillo. If that sounds good, any and all of it can also be ordered online. The cantina decor inside and on the multiple colorful outdoor patios, however, is half the reason to come here.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Mi Tradición
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: In Wooten, the brightly-colored Mi Tradición uses its house-made bread as the base for hefty Mexican tortas. Try the Chilanga with milanesa, ham, and chorizo, or go for the Hawaiana if pineapple is your thing. Every sandwich brings a rich mix of textures and bold flavors. There’s also a second location off William Cannon in South Austin. Grab a Mexican pastry from the case for later.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Moreno Barbecue
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: For under-the-radar barbecue with a minimal line, head to the Garrison Park restaurant, which fires up a variety of meats like brisket, pork belly, smoked half chickens, and Sunday barbacoa. Pick your meats to pile into sandwiches or tacos, or try the super-rich brisket grilled cheese for a decadent barbecue meal. Sides include the usual suspects — gouda mac and cheese, creamed corn, and coleslaw.
article_titles: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Mousam Spice House
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Mousam Spice House, with locations at North Campus, South Austin, and North Austin, is perfect for anyone looking for South Asian foods with a touch of Mexican style. There are burritos stuffed with paneer and chicken, tacos with paneer or chicken, and Chicken 65 that’s deep-fried, spiced and served with a house chutney.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Mum Foods
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: A cross between a Texas barbecue joint and an East Coast deli, Windsor Park’s Mum Foods smokes its own brisket, pastrami, and turkey, giving each sandwich serious depth. Go simple with egg salad or smoked chicken salad, or go big with the Rachel, which is piled high with slaw, Swiss, and Russian dressing.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Murray’s Tavern
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Travis Tober’s East Austin hotspot leans into old-school tavern style with a serious respect for steak. Murray’s Tavern serves a tender, rare prime rib after 4 p.m., Monday through Saturday, and a classic 8-ounce New York strip all day. Both are right at home alongside Grandma Murray’s Rob Roy, a scotch cocktail that’s as no-nonsense as the bar’s vibe. Don’t miss the $35 steak frites and martini night on Wednesdays.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Nasha
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Indian food with influences from Texas and Mexico can be found at this East Austin restaurant. Look for spicy curries, paneer quesadillas, keema (ground lamb) enchiladas, and South Asian tacos with fillings like chicken Tandoori and lamb masala. The fusion approach carries over into the margarita menu with a curried version, a cardamon one, and a pomegranate option.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Nate’s Baked Goods & Coffee
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: Nate’s is a great way to start your morning near Downtown, if you know where to look. Though it may be overshadowed by its neighbor, Better Half, this small breakfast and lunch spot has quality pastries like housemade muffins and scones, fluffy biscuits, and weekend kolaches and danishes. The savory options include a chicken salad sandwich with avocado and bacon that has quite a following.
article_titles: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Neighborhood Sushi
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: On your first visit, you might be walking around in circles in a bit of a daze, glancing up from your chosen map app, muttering, “is this it?” Neighborhood Sushi is hidden really well on South Congress in a back alley, but once you finally open those doors, you’re transported inside a minimalist serene escape from Austin’s busiest street.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: New Sitara Indian Cuisine
address: 1779 Wells Branch Pkwy Suite 102, Austin, TX 78728, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: The Wells Branch restaurant serves great Indian food for lunch and dinner with an Indian buffet. New Sitara’s tempting dishes will make it difficult to avoid second and third helpings. There is so much to sample, including Goan shrimp, all sorts of tandoori proteins from paneer to chicken wings, biryanis, and rasmalai (a cheese-based sweet).
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
