name: New World Deli
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Hyde Park’s New World Deli keeps it classic with a menu of cold cuts, grilled sandwiches, soups, and burgers in a charming old-school counter-service setting. The Reuben comes with corned beef, pastrami, turkey, or turkey pastrami, so you can tailor it to your preferences. The curried chicken salad sandwich is unreasonably good. Yes, pickup orders are available online.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Ng Cafe
address: 13000 North Interstate Highway 35 #200 Bldg 12, Austin, TX 78753, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: At Scofield Farms, Ng Cafe serves up some of the city’s best banh mi, featuring fresh baguettes baked on-site each morning that steal the show from all its other sandwiches, thanks to the crisp crust and soft center. Fillings include pâté, ham, rib-eye, and shredded chicken. Settle into the cozy, cute interiors with your satisfying sando. Online orders are welcome.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Nido
address: Rooftop, 1211 W Riverside Dr, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Arguably, the most picturesque views of Downtown come from across the river, at the Loren Hotel at Lady Bird Lake. The Zilker restaurant’s patio has a beautiful view of the lake, the sparkling skyscrapers of the cityscape, and the rolling hills beyond. There’s also an indoor dining room and cocktail lounge, but the best views are on the patio.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Oasis Texas Brewing Company
notes: Austin Restaurants With Great Views: Locals know to go to this brewery/restaurant out on far northwest Austin for nachos, burgers, and beers, plus a spot-on view of Lake Travis. This laid-back spot is popular for its stunning sunset views and live music. The restaurant features a large patio space and an indoor area, but it closes for inclement weather.
article_titles: Austin Restaurants With Great Views

name: Odd Duck
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Odd Duck was a leader in the farm-to-table revolution in Austin when it opened in 2009, and is still making some of the best food in the city with a creative, ever-changing menu of small plates from chef Bryce Gilmore.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Odd Duck
address: 1201 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Enjoy fancy Texan cuisine in a welcoming environment from notable Austin chef Bryce Gilmore and his team. The restaurant has been killing it since 2013, and while some aspects of the place may seem dated (small plates served on mismatched china), the food and service have only gotten better. Gilmore works closely with local farmers to showcase seasonal ingredients on a constantly evolving menu.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Otherside Deli
address: 1104 W 34th St Ste D, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Also serving killer pastrami, Otherside Deli in Old West Austin builds standout pastrami and corned beef sandwiches that hit all the right notes. The meats are juicy and sliced thick, and served on rye or soft rolls that hold up under pressure. It’s a no-frills counter-service joint with big flavor and serious sandwich chops. The turkey sandwich is also worthy of an order (which you can place online).
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Papercut
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: Papercut takes bar décor to another level. The artwork on the walls isn’t just for show — the rotating works by local artists directly inspire its drinks menu. In the back, you’ll find Konbini, a collaboration with the Michelin-recognized Tare team, serving a tight menu of sushi. You don’t have a lot of opportunities to have good sushi with outstanding cocktails in Austin (most places don’t have a full bar).
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Patika
address: 100 Congress Ave Ste 125, Austin, TX, 78701, US
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: Alas, Patika closed down its flagship cafe on South Lamar but thankfully, its spirit lives on with its downtown location within an office building lobby. There are its thoughtful coffees (the vanilla latte remains one of the best in town) as well as house-made baked goods and hearty daytime dishes. Takeout orders are placed online or in person.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Patrizi’s
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: House-made pastas anchor the menu of this food truck at the Vortex theater and bar in Cherrywood. Have a very Austin date night on the whimsical patio or inside the funky indoor space with chewy fettuccine in Karah’s Diavolo, a lemon-olive-oil-garlic-rosemary-chile mix topped with an egg yolk. Sides like beef and pork meatballs and roasted beets round out the menu.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Patty Palace
must_try: The fried onion petals, which are served with a side of Zesty sauce.
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: With two locations — one at St. Elmo Brewing down South and another at Zilker Brewing on the East Side — this food truck is all about smash burgers. The namesake burger layers two griddled patties with sharp cheddar, a smoky Royal sauce, grilled onions, and just enough crunch from house pickles.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

address: 1400 S. Congress Suite B100, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The vibe at this Bouldin Creek seafood restaurant is clean, polished, and rich, with signature blue and white umbrellas and fancy fonts. It’s one of Austin’s original high-end patio dining experiences, and still one of the best. Oysters, Champagne cocktails, and fresh seafood a la plancha are worth the wait, plus there’s a prime view of South Congress.

It’s part of the McGuire Moorman Lambert family of restaurants, so expect to pay for the impeccable aesthetics.

name: Pinthouse Pizza
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The brewery/pizzeria mini-chain has become a mainstay of both family dinners and beer nerds by offering a convivial, communal atmosphere paired with a comfort food menu of pizza, nachos, soft pretzels, and (critically) award-winning beers. The multi-location brewery is best known for the ubiquitous Electric Jellyfish IPA, a hazy New England-style beer that’s served everywhere in Austin.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Pinthouse Pizza [Burnet]
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Pinthouse brews up some of the most popular beers in Austin – shoutout Electric Jellyfish IPA – and its four friendly, bustling locations are go-to spots for families, big social groups, after-work happy hours, and just about any other kind of casual gathering. But the pizza here is not just of the “meet your needs” variety.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Poeta
address: 1108 East 6th Street, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: After a stint as a prix fixe restaurant, Poeta, the Michelin-recommended sibling restaurant to Intero, moved into East Austin Hotel in January. There are a la carte options like milk-braised pork and lasagne (the prix fixe menu will return in late February). Poeta brunch means the return of the infamous “wafflegato”— a waffle served with gelato and espresso butter.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Pool Burger
address: 2315 Lake Austin Blvd, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Hidden behind Deep Eddy Cabaret in Tarrytown, Pool Burger feels like a breezy beach shack that’s pineapple-scented and rum-soaked. The Blue Hawaiian burger adds a thick grilled pineapple slice to a beef patty and Thousand Island dressing, which pairs well with the crinkle-cut fries for $14. It’s playful and well-executed.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Proud Mary Coffee
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: An import from Australia, Proud Mary is paradise for coffee snobs. The cafe offers fancy pour overs and espresso, along with brunch fare like a sausage roll, ricotta hotcakes, and avocado toast. The interior is light and airy, with soaring ceilings and light wood, and there’s a small, rustic outdoor patio. Order takeout if necessary—it can get busy.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Pueblo Viejo Riverside
address: 2410 E Riverside Dr Ste H8, Austin, TX, 78741, US
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: This East Riverside taco spot — one of several Pueblo Viejo locations across Austin and its only brick-and-mortar — is a go-to for fast, full-plate lunch. Whether craving al pastor tacos or a quesadilla plate with rice and beans, the portions are solid, and the salsas pack heat. There’s covered seating on-site, or take your order to go. Open daily from 7 a.m. to 10 p.m.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Quattro Gatti
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: A hidden gem by the Capitol on Congress Avenue, Quattro Gatti is a great option for downtown diners seeking Italian dishes. The menu includes pizzas blasted in a wood-fired oven for a perfectly chewy crust, traditional pastas, and hefty entrees. Don’t skip the creamy gelato for dessert.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: R&B’s Steak and Fries
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: East Austin’s R&B’s cranks out cheesesteaks loaded with thin-sliced ribeye and your choice of Cheez Whiz, Provolone, or American cheese (go with Whiz). Each sandwich arrives hot on an Amoroso roll with plenty of melty goodness inside. Outdoor seating provides a space to dive right in. It is just as yummy when you order online.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Radio/East
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: The crown jewel of the Radio Coffee and Beer empire, this massive coffee bar on Montopolis Drive strikes a balance between being a cool spot to co-work during the day, inside or on the patio, and co-thrive with live music at night.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Some of the state’s best ramen can be found over at Ramen Tatsu-ya. Choose from ramen made with pork bone broth, chicken shoyu broth, or the almond-milk-and-mushroom combination for a vegan option, and add a spicy bomb if you’re adventurous. There are also small bites like gyoza and karaage, and everything is available for dine-in or to go.

name: Ramos Tex-Mex Restaurant
address: 14611 N Mopac Expy #103, Austin, TX 78728, USA
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: This Wells Branch restaurant’s menu reads like a dictionary of Tex-Mex favorites. Chimichangas stuffed with roasted chicken, green chiles, and smothered in sauce; tender pork marinated in red chile; steak a la Mexicana; fajitas; tortilla soup; fried ice cream; and numerous combination plates.
article_titles: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin

name: Red Ash
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: The scents of wood-fire-grilled steaks and red snapper a la plancha lure diners into this modern two-story downtown Italian restaurant. But even with many tables in the restaurant, reservations should be made well in advance. Locally sourced meats and an assortment of seafood are roasted on a custom-built wood-burning grill and oven.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Rocco’s Neighborhood Joint
address: 5001 Airport Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78751, United States
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: Restaurateur Wade McElroy (Love Supreme Pizza and Teddy’s Cocktail Bar) and the former executive chef at Sammie’s, Nicholas Ford, opened the doors to Rocco’s Neighborhood Joint in September on Airport Boulevard. The spirit of the spot is meant to channel casual Italian eateries you might find in the Northeast with a small menu of antipasto, salads, pastas, and Italian-only wines.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Rock N Grill
address: 1702 N Mays St Suite A, Round Rock, TX 78664, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: At this Round Rock restaurant, find Southern and Northern Indian food, including goat and mutton dishes to pakoras (fritters), spicy fried fish to tandoori (grilled meats), paneer entrees, and dosas (savory thin South Asian pancakes basically).
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Roya
address: 7858 Shoal Creek Boulevard, STE C, Austin, Texas 78757, United States
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Chef Amir Hajimaleki’s Persian restaurant Roya has been in progress since 2018, and in February it finally opened in its full glory. Located on top of one of Hajimaleki’s other restaurants, District Kitchen + Cocktails, the rich blue space sets a sumptuous tone with intricate tilework, art, and unique chandeliers.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The Japanese-ish Govalle cafe is serving up espresso drinks alongside the saifa (its take on Vietnamese coffee), plus cold brew. There are also larger Japanese daytime entrees like katsus and loaded toasts. It has a large, industrial space with exposed cinder block walls, rustic tables, and loads of plants, while there outdoor tables offer some sunlight. Takeout is available online.

notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: More Indian and Nepalese specialties — plus some Indo-Chinese takes — can be found at this Northwest Hills restaurant, which continues to be a favorite amongst Austinites. Its namesake spice, saffron, shows up in the goat curry, scallops, chicken lazeez, and in the mixed tandoori grill. The rest of the menu is spared the seasoning, but it is still plenty spiced and flavorful.

name: Sagebrush
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Noticed the glitzy sign that says “Sagebrush” while stuck in traffic on South Congress heading south? Head inside this bougie meets backwater bar, where the space expands from the tiny facade with a vaulted ceiling over the dance floor into red velvet curtains framing the stage, and blue velvet walls acting as the background for the pool tables. A mesa sunset illusion illuminates the other side of the seating area.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food

name: Sahara Lounge
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: This place likely has the liveliest buffet you’ll ever experience. Sahara Lounge’s Africa night on Saturdays is an absolute must to experience Afrobeat dance tunes and a buffet lineup typically featuring spiced meatballs and chicken, bean stew, vegetable rice, and tortilla chips, starting at 7 p.m.

notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: This Jersey-proud red sauce joint from vibe-conscious hospitality group McGuire Moorman Lambert offers Italian American classics in an upscale atmosphere downtown. Look for dishes like chicken Parmigiana, spaghetti and meatballs from the chef’s family recipe, and smothered pork chops with maitake mushrooms and a marsala brandy cream sauce.

name: San Ginés [Austin]
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: Take a mental trip to Spain with this all-day restaurant known for its churros. The back patio is gorgeous, with lots of plants, Spanish language tunes, umbrellas, comfy couches, large work tables, and a few fire pits. It’s open from coffee and pastries in the morning to salad and cava with lunch to evening cocktails. If the weather doesn’t cooperate, the indoor bar and seating area is equally vibey.

name: Sandy’s Hamburgers
address: 603 Barton Springs Rd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: Tucked just south of the river off Barton Springs Road, Sandy’s is part-burger stand, part-time capsule. The restaurant makes for an excellent post-Barton Springs lunch spot: The old-fashioned, no-frills cheeseburger hits all the right notes — hot, salty, and just a little bit messy — and costs about $6. Adding a malted shake (try the butterscotch or Oreo) or dipped frozen custard cone is a non-negotiable.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Sangam Chettinad Indian Cuisine
address: 6001 W Parmer Ln #140, Austin, TX 78727, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: While the Milwood restaurant offers dishes from the entire country of India, its specialties lean more towards Southern Indian, especially Chettinad cuisine from the Tamil Nadu state. The breakfast menu includes egg dosas, idiyappam (rice noodles) paired with coconut milk, and potato masalas.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Sap's Fine Thai Cuisine
address: 4514 West Gate Blvd, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: The Westgate Thai restaurant from Sap Apisaksari is a result of his divorce from Chatfuang Mam, who kept their original restaurant, Madame Mam’s. Expect curries, noodle soups, and fiery stir-fries like the spicy long beans, each prepared with attention to heat and balance. Fans keep returning to the roomy, casual spot for consistently tasty dishes and variety.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

Today, it continues to serve sausages, schnitzel, and pretzels alongside cold drinks. Its spacious patio remains a festive gathering place for football games and community events.

name: Space Kat BBQ
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Space Kat opened in March serving smoked meats out of an offbeat converted schoolbus on Webberville Road, but — is this even possible in Texas? — there’s no brisket on the menu. Instead, pitmaster Jack Zizzo offers cuts like tri-tip, lamb ribs, hot links, and coppa along with expected sides like mac and cheese and coleslaw.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: St. Elmo Brewing Company
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: While best known for its crisp Carl Kolsch, the East Congress brewery brews a wide array of styles with range: there are solid IPAs, a Japanese rice lager, and even a fruity sour optionFood from Spicy Boys is available, and there are coolers of tallboy cans to go. There is also a second location with a large play area for kids, and both locations have indoor and outdoor seating.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

Conor Smith helms the bakery, which churns out fresh breads, croissants, and pastries throughout the day — check the bake schedule online and don’t miss the pillowy soft cinnamon with tangy icing.

name: Suprabhat
address: 9225 W Parmer Ln Suite 108, Austin, TX 78717, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Suprabhat in Davis Spring focuses on vegetarian dishes from across India. There are plenty of chaats, such as chopped samosas, along with idlis (thick rice cakes) topped with chutneys, and so many dosas. There’s also a section dedicated to Jain cuisines, where the dishes are made without onions and garlic.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Tancho Sushi
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: There’s no shortage of exclusive, high-priced sushi establishments in Austin, which is why Tancho’s motto is “Omakase for everyone.” With two locations — one in West Oak Hill, and another in Rosedale — Tancho isn’t trying to reinvent the experience or offer gimmicks to get you in the door. Instead, they’re focused on fresh fish without many of the frills that typically accompany a high-dollar omakase.

name: Tandoori Lounge
address: 3601 W William Cannon Dr #450, Austin, TX 78749, USA
notes: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin: Southcross Plaza’s South Asian restaurant is centered on tandoori of all sorts, where spiced items are cooked in a special clay oven, including lamb, goat, shrimp, paneer, chicken, and pomplet — a type of fish cooked bone-in. There are also more general pan-Indian dishes like samosas, koftas, aloo gobi, curries, and some Indo-Chinese specialties cooked up and served in this bar-meets-biryani restaurant.
article_titles: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin

name: Taqueria de Diez (South Lamar)
address: 2110 South Lamar Boulevard, STE C, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: The original location of this Taqueria de Diez is a kind of taco speakeasy downtown, but the secret’s out with this expansion to South Lamar. Taqueria de Diez serves simple but awesome tacos, quesadillas, and burritos, like a succulent al pastor and meaty carne asada. It’s also a great option for any late-night taco cravings, open until 1 a.m. Thursday through Saturday.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Taquito Aviles
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: In a city with as many taco options as Austin, it’s hard to choose hidden gem spots. Aside from the breakfast tacos, the goat options at this casual North Austin taqueria truck make it a standout. Try the rich birria de chivo taco or the spicy consomé de chivo with handmade corn tortillas. Take your tacos to go, or sit at one of the few shaded picnic tables and scarf down your meal al fresco.
article_titles: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: Tare
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: This Austin omakase restaurant within the North Star neighborhood focuses on meshing Japanese, South Texan, and Mexican flavors and ingredients throughout its kappo-style Japanese menu.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Taste of Ethiopia
address: 3801 S Congress Ave #107, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: South Congress is home to the second location of this casual Ethiopian restaurant that’s especially popular with vegetarians. Expect hearty stews like doro wot (chicken with berbere spices) and yemisir wot (slow-cooked lentils), all served atop wonderfully spongy injera bread. The interior dining room is not the most impressive in the city, but the hospitality is some of the friendliest.

address: 2700 W Anderson Ln Ste 212, Austin, TX, 78757, US
notes: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin: While its location in a North Austin strip mall might not be anything remarkable, step inside Tatsumi and you’ll be treated to an exciting array of high-low options, from affordable classics like the spicy tuna roll, to the indulgent Ebi Uni Maguro stuffed with charred A5 Miyazaki beef, and more decadent and inventive dishes like the Negi Toro Toast topped with matcha powder, kaluga caviar, and brown butter.
article_titles: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin

name: Texas Chili Parlor
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Diners at Texas Chili Parlor can choose from a wide array of chili options — some with pork, some with and without beans — fueling the city’s long-running debate whether or not beans belong in chili. The restaurant has been a staple since 1976, predating the time when chili was officially the state dish; a mention in a Guy Clark song has only emphasized its enduring charm.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Thai Fresh
address: 909 W Mary St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: The Bouldin Creek spot from chef and owner Jam Sanitchat does triple duty as a restaurant, cafe, and bakery that focuses on Texas-sourced sustainable ingredients. The cozy South Austin spot offers traditional dishes like papaya salad and green curry fried rice. The adjacent market stocks herbs, spices, and curry pastes for home cooking.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Thai Thani
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: This food truck on a busy Cherrywood street, serves curries, stir-fries, noodles, and standout halal chicken wings. The wings are crispy and well-seasoned, and the curries and noodles pack rich flavors. The casual setup makes it easy to grab a quick, but satisfying meal.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: The 04 Lounge
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: The Dawson neighborhood bar off South Congress Avenue is small and dark with a side patio. There are several pool tables and arcade games. The drinks are cheap. The regulars are friendly. It’s a quintessential Austin dive bar to its core. ‘Nuff said.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: The Aristocrat Lounge
notes: The Best Dive Bars in Austin: The bright pink walls and poodle decorations are a beacon for those seeking this Burnet Road dive bar. Formerly the Poodle Lounge, Aristocrat provides a comfortable atmosphere, affordable drinks, and a straightforward good time. It features multiple pool tables, a jukebox, a shuffleboard, and a small back patio with custom furniture crafted from old truck beds.
article_titles: The Best Dive Bars in Austin

name: The Brew and Brew
address: 500 San Marcos St Ste 105, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The East Austin coffee shop has an elegant menu of espresso, filter coffee, and cold brews from local roasters. It is light and warm, and friendly baristas can guide the uninitiated through the latest and greatest trends in the coffee world. It serves sandwiches and bagels for the daytime, while the evening menu has burgers, fries, and chicken sandwiches. Plus there’s a full bar and loads of beers and wines.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: The Brewtorium
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The Skyview brewpub succeeds by blending the old and the new. There’s a focus on traditional European beers like the schwarzbier (a dark lager) and the Czech pilsner, but that doesn’t mean you can’t order a hazy IPA or a barrel-aged sour instead. There’s also a welcoming beer hall setup, plus a small outdoor area.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: The Butterfly Bar
notes: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin: The Butterfly Bar is named that because it is an actual butterfly sanctuary. The Blackland bar has eclectic decor, cocktails, beers, wines, and Patrizi’s food truck which serves some of the best pasta in town — plus there are outdoor cats. What more could you need? It’s owned by nonprofit theater company the Vortex, so there are often performances and shows to enjoy, and drink purchases support local art.
article_titles: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin

name: The Carillon
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Tucked inside the AT&T Hotel and Conference Center, the Carillon serves one of Austin’s most underrated dining experiences. The seasonal menu features dishes like Gulf seafood crudo, compressed watermelon and ricotta salads, and lamb chops with smoked pistachio butter, blending inventive techniques with familiar flavors.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: The Far Out Lounge & Stage
address: 8504 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food: Don’t let the tiny stone building fool you. Once inside the bar, the backyard at the Far Out Lounge opens up to a large expanse of shaded lawn and a stage for its rotating list of musical acts. While there are a few seats inside, you’ll likely want to grab a spot outside to catch the live performances. Chow down on a bite from Knuckle Sandwich while you do.
article_titles: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
hours_match_name: The Far Out Lounge and Stage

name: The Little Darlin’
address: 6507 Circle S Rd, Austin, TX, 78745, US
notes: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin: If the Little Darlin’ weren’t so far south, it would be one of the most popular bars in Austin. It nails all the essential bar elements — stellar drinks and solid food, including a hefty burger, a cold udon noodle salad for a perfect lunch, and an extra-rich macaroni and cheese made with three cheeses.
article_titles: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin

name: The Meteor
address: 2110 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Anyone looking to grab a bottle of wine, a slice of pizza, and/or bike gear will find a home at the Meteor, which is an extremely casual counter-service cafe, bottle shop, and bike shop. It offers coffee, pizza, breakfast sandwiches, and grain bowls. Order online to skip the line, though bike chains aren’t available to go.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: ThunderCloud Subs
address: 2021 E Riverside Dr, Austin, TX 78741, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: The Austin-based ThunderCloud chain keeps things fast, fresh, and surprisingly good. The California Club with turkey, avocado, and bacon always satisfies. Sandwiches come piled high and served with a side of nostalgia. Many locations offer a drive-through for added convenience. Obviously, don’t skip the Thunder Sauce. Order online for pickup at all its locations.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Tiny Boxwoods
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: In Brykerwoods, Tiny Boxwoods turns lunch into a garden party in its adorable, light-filled dining room. From 11 a.m. to 2:30 p.m., you’ll find salads with roasted chicken, avocado toast, and the famous chocolate chip cookies that definitely do count as a meal. The setting is polished but breezy, with whitewashed brick, soft lighting, and a patio that always seems to have just the right breeze.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Titaya's Thai Cuisine
address: 5501 N Lamar Blvd Ste C101, Austin, TX 78751, USA
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: North Lamar’s longtime Thai favorite delivers on bold flavors and an expansive menu. Standouts include Ta-Lay Dancing, a spicy stir-fry of shrimp, squid, green mussels, and vegetables, alongside curries and noodles. With its bustling dining room and steady following for over 15 years, Titaya’s is a cornerstone of Austin’s Thai scene.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Toasty Badger
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: This family-friendly, retro-by-the-way-of-the-1970s diner with East Coast vibes offers cheery murals, counter seating, and a menu full of cozy breakfast hits. Order towering pancakes, eggs all day, or get lunchy with a Monte Cristo or fried chicken sandwich. The house coffee’s strong, lending to a neighborhood hangout that feels like it’s always been there.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Top Notch
must_try: Top Notch is also known for its fried foods, including fried chicken, fish, and shrimp, sides like fried okra and hand-battered onion rings, and fried apple and cherry pies for dessert.
notes: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas: In Crestview, Top Notch still charcoal-grills its burgers like it’s 1971. The drive-in setup and vintage signage haven’t changed much since Dazed and Confused made it a cult favorite, and neither has the burger, which has remained thick, smoky, and deeply satisfying. The prices are nostalgic, too, with burgers for around $6 and combos for about $12.
article_titles: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas

name: Trattoria Lisina
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: It’s worth the trek out to Driftwood for a quick getaway from reality at this Hill Country restaurant on a sprawling vineyard. Wander the on-site garden, which recalls the Italian countryside, before entering the spacious trattoria, which has a bustling open kitchen and large fireplace.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Trianon Coffee
address: 3654 Bee Caves Rd Ste A, West Lake Hills, TX, 78746, US
notes: The Best Coffee in Austin: The classic Westlake coffee shop offers all sorts of coffees from single-origin to microlots, and bites from Rosen’s Bagels, Tacodeli, and Quack’s. The colorful shop has comfy armchairs, tables for two, a high top for laptop warriors, and a private room for groups or meetings. Or take it to go in person or order from Uber Eats and GrubHub.
article_titles: The Best Coffee in Austin

name: Tropicana Cuban Restaurant
address: 9616 N Lamar Blvd #141, Austin, TX 78753, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: North Austin’s Tropicana dishes out pressed Cuban sandwiches that are hot and melty. The Cubano, medianoche, and tortas all start with house-baked bread that gets crisped to order. Juicy pork, smoky ham, and melted Swiss cheese combine to create a hearty, flavorful sandwich. Order a Cuban espresso to finish the meal strong at this festive strip-mall spot. It accepts online orders.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Tucci's Southside Subs
address: 801 E William Cannon Dr, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Tucci’s serves hefty Italian subs, either cold or toasted. Try the spicy Italian hoagie, grilled eggplant, or hot ham and Swiss for a filling bite. The full-size sandwiches are generous, so consider ordering a half unless you plan to have a second meal. Both South Austin locations offer indoor seating and easy online ordering. Pickup and delivery orders are online.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Tuk Tuk Thai Cafe
notes: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin: South Manchaca’s Tuk Tuk Thai Cafe is a family-run restaurant beloved for its homestyle cooking. The menu features curries, stir-fries, and soups, with house specials like hor mok, a steamed fish in red curry, stealing the spotlight. Warm service and a cozy, lived-in space give it the feel of a neighborhood fixture.
article_titles: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin

name: Tweedy’s Bar
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: The spirit of the old Spider House lives on in Tweedy’s Bar. Vintage decor and rock ’n’ roll energy shape the space, created by the team behind Hotel Vegas and Yellow Jacket Social Club. Cocktails, like classic daiquiris, margaritas, and bloody marys, as well as beer and wine, anchor the menu, while nearby food trucks Li’l Nonna’s, Yalla Burger, Zucchini Kill, and Guppy’s offer sustenance.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Twin Isle
notes: The Best New Restaurants in Austin: Janelle Romeo, the chef who ran Shirley’s Trini Cuisine food truck, has opened Twin Isle at 1401 Rosewood Avenue in the spot formerly occupied by Project Pollo. The fast-casual restaurant serves flavors from her native Trinidad and Tobago, offering roti wraps, customizable bowls, jerk chicken, oxtail sandwiches, and more Caribbean classics.
article_titles: The Best New Restaurants in Austin

name: Uchi
notes: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin: Tyson Cole opened this Japanese restaurant on South Lamar back in 2003, when sushi was still new to landlocked Austin. It’s been a hit ever since, earning the chef a James Beard Award and spawning an empire across the country. The restaurant serves fresh, sustainable, and responsibly fished ingredients in dishes like its signature hama chili (yellowtail with ponzu, thai chili, and orange) and wagyu beef hot rock.
article_titles: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin

name: Uchiba
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Uchibā adds a refined midday option to the Downtown lineup, with lunch served daily from 11:30 a.m. to 2:30 p.m. Highlights include the sushi oma with hama chili, the tuna hand roll, and the ba lunch — a set featuring sesame ramen salad, a salmon hand roll, and two pieces of nigiri alongside a choice of karaage, pork dumplings, or a hot fried chicken bun.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Uchiko
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: Omakases may have taken over the country, and Uchiko may have expanded into a multi-state empire, but chef Tyson Cole’s second restaurant in Rosedale is still serving some of the best Japanese fare in Austin 15 years after its opening. Exceptional bites like simply garnished madai, the classic hama chili, and the cult-favorite milk and cereal dessert make up the very fabric of Austin dining.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Umarell
address: 4413 Guadalupe Street, Austin, Texas 78751, United States
notes: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin: After working at a high-end Emmer & Rye restaurant, chef Mike Graffeo wanted to create a place true to his New Jersey roots. This casual counter-service spot in Hyde Park serves all the red sauce classics: a big-as-your-head chicken parm sandwich, rigatoni and meatballs in a tomato cream sauce, and end piece lasagne fried in garlic oil until crispy.
article_titles: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin

name: Uncle Nicky's
notes: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin: Set along a busy stretch in Hyde Park, Uncle Nicky’s brings Euro-café flair to the neighborhood with pastas and sandwiches stuffed with meatballs, porchetta, and chicken Parmesan, all of which pair well with its crisp salads. The interiors are clean and casual with counter service and a small patio. Save room for a pastry or gelato; don’t forget to fulfill your espresso fix. The all-day/lunch menu starts at 11 a.m.
article_titles: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin

name: Uptown Sports Club
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Aaron Franklin’s relaxed all-day Eastside eatery serves po’ boys with a New Orleans spirit and an Austin touch. Fried shrimp, roast beef, and fried green tomatoes each get a turn in the spotlight. The sandwiches feel indulgent but stay balanced. Pair one with a cocktail and settle in for a long lunch or festive dinner. Take it to go or order online.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Usta Kababgy
must_try: The lahmajun (meat flatbread) is a filling, ripe-for-any-time comfort food.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: With locations in North Austin and Round Rock, this casual Middle Eastern restaurant focuses on delectable halal grilled meats. The menu boasts a bevy of kebab options, such as lamb-and-beef adana and chicken, as well as shawarma bowls, wraps, falafels, and dolmas. Inside, find booth seating and friendly service. Online to-go ordering is available.
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: VanHorn’s
notes: The Best Steakhouses in Austin: Big Apple glamour takes center stage at this New York-inspired steakhouse, where the team behind Bill’s Oyster and Teddy’s Restaurant & Bar pairs wood panels and red leather booths with stiff martinis. The menu sticks to the classics, with the focus squarely on the steaks, including prime dry-aged cuts sourced from Pat LaFrieda Meat Purveyors.
article_titles: The Best Steakhouses in Austin

name: Vaquero Taquero
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Dig into Rio Grande Valley tacos and quesadillas, which feature fillings like al pastor and bistec, as well as vegetarian options like nopales and rajas poblanas. The straightforward setup keeps attention on the vibrant flavors coming out of the kitchen. Bonus: The Downtown location also doubles as a music venue and cantina.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Veracruz All Natural
open_for: Breakfast, lunch, dinner at some locations
must_try: Decide between the migas or a migas poblanas taco.
notes: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026: At this essential Austin food truck, sisters Reyna and Maritza Vazquez make some of the best tacos in the city, served on handmade corn and flour tortillas with fresh salsas (the latter piled in a grab-as-many-as-you-need cooler).
article_titles: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026

name: Vespaio
address: 1610 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin: Vespaio gives the feel of an old-school Italian restaurant without being too stuffy, with lots of dark wood, a wine wall, and fresh handmade pasta, plus a couple of tables outside. The menu is classically Italian-American, with dishes like cioppino, cacio e pepe, and pizza. Though the restaurant is more romantic, order to-go for an at-home date night.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin

name: Via 313
notes: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin: Detroit-style pizza finds a home at Via 313 in North Campus. The square pies with thick, crisp crusts come loaded with toppings, like the Detroiter with double pepperoni, while the Caesar salad offers a lighter but equally satisfying option. The laid-back atmosphere makes it a dependable stop for groups of friends.
article_titles: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin

name: Via 313 [East 6th Restaurant]
notes: The Best Pizza in Austin: Price: $$ Once just a single food truck on East 6th dishing some of the only quality Detroit pies in Austin, Via 313 has grown into a behemoth of a local chain, with several dine-in locations across the greater Austin region, including ’burbs like Cedar Park, as well as spots in Houston, San Antonio, and even out west in Utah and Colorado.
article_titles: The Best Pizza in Austin

name: Vic and Al’s
address: 2406 Manor Rd Ste D, Austin, TX, 78722, US
notes: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin: Cherrywood’s Vic & Al’s brings Cajun flavor to its loaded po’boys. The cochon de lait packs in slow-roasted pork layered with all the fixings that make this sandwich worthy of taking a nap after eating. The bread holds firm, while the fillings stay rich and juicy. It’s comfort food with a Louisiana drawl.
article_titles: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin

name: Vista Brewing
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: Much like Jester King, Vista Brewing is best viewed as a destination brewery: the drive out to the 20 acre property in Driftwood is part of the fun. Don’t skip the food, which makes use of local ranchers and an on-site farm for dishes like pickled veggies, kids fare, and juicy burgers, which are just $15 with a beer on Thursdays.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area

name: Vivo
address: 6406 N Interstate Hwy 35 Ste 2343, Austin, TX 78752, USA
notes: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin: The Linc Tex-Mex restaurant is steeped in San Antonio traditions, which means puffy tacos (beef picadillo, shredded chicken, shrimp, and beans and vegetables are among the filling options) alongside enchiladas and fajita tacos on the menu. However, Vivo is mostly known for its colorful, creative margaritas, including options with watermelon and cucumber.

name: Zilker Brewing Co.
notes: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area: The East Sixth brewery has become an East Austin staple by producing crowd-pleasing beers — mostly in lighter styles — and canning quite a few for backyard and picnic consumption. While the interior of the taproom is fairly small due to the central location, there’s a generous patio to perch on, complete with fried chicken (and tempeh) sandwiches and tots from the on-site truck Spicy Boys.
article_titles: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
